web.title: 14 Vintage Architectural Features That Are Making a Serious ...
web.description: Explore 14 timeless architectural features designers are reviving to add character, depth, and modern elegance to today's homes.
web.markdown:
[![House Beautiful](https://hermes.media.static.aol.com/logos/light/aol_house_beautiful_888.png)](http://www.housebeautiful.com/)

Yelena Moroz-Alpert

Mon, June 29, 2026 at 4:32 PM UTC

0

![Cozy living space featuring framed art, a red armchair, and an inviting green window seat with cushions and plants.](https://hermes.media.static.aol.com/media/2026/06/29/6cc2a8e0-2d63-37f7-8766-9fef12f3bc24/b99effa9-e443-4f07-a33d-c56a4bb192a1.jpg)

Vintage Design Details That Are Making a ComebackNick Johnson

_"Hearst Magazines and AOL may earn commission or revenue on some items through these links."_

Today’s design style is less a rigid aesthetic than a sensibility. It's an interpretation—an effortless blend of tastes, periods, and influences that feels cohesive the moment you step inside. Today, designers from coast to coast are revisiting [vintage architectural elements](https://www.housebeautiful.com/design-inspiration/a71051015/historic-home-design-details/) and details, incorporating them in ways that are both unexpected and distinctly contemporary.

“We're seeing a renewed appreciation for elements that bring depth, character, and a sense of permanence to a space,” says Tamara Kaye-Honey, principal and founder of [House of Honey](http://www.houseofhoney.com/) in South Pasadena and Montecito, California. “These elements tell a story and give a home a sense of soul that transcends trends.”

Take the vertical wooden slats that first appeared in the 1940s and became a staple into the 1970s, for instance. The partitions found a revival with the infiltration of the [Japandi style](https://www.housebeautiful.com/room-decorating/a45851530/japandi-interior-design-style/). To keep these partitions from looking passé or too midcentury, New York designer [Louis Lin](https://www.studiolouislin.com/) layers the wood with other architectural details from different time periods, combining these with classical dentil molding and Danish-inspired casework, so the final look is “interpretive and abstract, not too themed or nostalgic.”

Dabbling in your own version of an architectural time machine involves seeking out classical elements that have withstood the test of time, surrounding them with _au courant_ flair—no one will ever say that the arched doorways and windows are a [tacky trend](https://www.housebeautiful.com/lifestyle/a71217880/outdated-home-trends-designers-hate/).

“Character homes were built with integrity and thoughtfulness, and it is our responsibility as designers to protect the detailed aspects of building as we work in this modern era,” adds Kirsten Blazek of [A1000XBetter](http://www.a1000xbetter.com/) in Southern California.

Take a look at 14 ways designers are using original, salvaged, and replicas of century-old staples to make their work more than just a blast from the past.

## Vertical Slats

![Modern living room with wooden shelves, curved chairs, and decorative elements.](https://hermes.media.static.aol.com/media/2026/06/29/6cc2a8e0-2d63-37f7-8766-9fef12f3bc24/0e9da503-0160-4048-bdc4-bf4d427f9f1d.jpg)

Design byStudio Louis Lin.Nicolas Schimp

In the above space, [Studio Louis Lin](https://www.studiolouislin.com/) played a bit of a design roulette by integrating linear [Art Deco elements](https://www.housebeautiful.com/design-inspiration/a69937807/art-deco-home-decor/) with midcentury modern Danish style, tying it all together with one architectural fixture: the wood room partition.

“Screens have long been used across interiors, from ancient palaces to midcentury rooms, because they separate spaces without fully closing them off,” says Lin. The vertical slat dividers are “part boundary, part portal” that works as an architectural element to create a transition without disruptive sight lines or a visual blockade.

## Arched Doorways

![Dining room with floral wallpaper and wooden table set seen through archway](https://hermes.media.static.aol.com/media/2026/06/29/6cc2a8e0-2d63-37f7-8766-9fef12f3bc24/53e4920f-b044-4ab8-91b8-42e63c8a917b.jpeg)

Design byA1000XBetter.Michael P.H. Clifford

You could say that designers have a certain loyalty to history and craftsmanship. That’s why the doorway arch—a nod to 1920s and 1930s Spanish Revival—is an architectural detail that often remains in older homes, and is now frequently introduced in newer builds. “There is a softness to an arched opening that is so appealing. An arched opening acts to invite you into a room,” says Blazek, who offset the silhouette with floral wallpaper from House of Hackney.

## Parquet Flooring

![Elegant hallway with floral wallpaper and marble sideboard.](https://hermes.media.static.aol.com/media/2026/06/29/6cc2a8e0-2d63-37f7-8766-9fef12f3bc24/044680ed-10fe-4159-9f02-f49c31571791.jpeg)

Design byDunham Robinson.Nicholas Venezia

Parquet flooring—translated as “little compartments”—found fame after Louis XIV had it installed in the Palace of Versailles in 1684. The Victorians and 19th-century industrialists popularized it among the wealthy masses. Over time, however, the darker, almost orange (and quite shiny!) finish gave way to more streamlined planks, but the checkerboard-like floor is making a comeback—with a twist.

One homeowner described the original parquet flooring as part of a house that was "wearing too much makeup," says New York designer Rachel Robinson, partner at [Dunham Robinson](http://dunhamrobinson.com/). Her studio wanted to retain the natural beauty but make the floors more contemporary by refinishing the floors and the wood trim, in a much lighter stain that gravitated from archaic and into refreshing territory.

## Stained Glass

![Purple flowers in a vase on a windowsill with leaded glass window](https://hermes.media.static.aol.com/media/2026/06/29/6cc2a8e0-2d63-37f7-8766-9fef12f3bc24/4090b309-b8ad-4646-bbb5-738a4547a1b1.jpg)

Design byBethany Adams.JL Jordan Photography

“I've designed a lot of historic homes over the past 20 years and I've definitely noticed a shift from, ‘What are we going to do to distract from all of this stained glass?’ to ‘How can we incorporate stained glass in our project?’" says Louisville, Kentucky, designer [Bethany Adams](https://www.bethanyadamsinteriors.com/).

“What I find the most interesting is that the clients clamoring for colored glass don't live in Victorian homes. It's newer or midcentury styles that are getting a glassy glow-up.” If you’re considering stained glass outside of a historical home, look for a space with plenty of natural light to amplify the effect.

## Beadboard Ceilings

![Cozy nook with two small tables, flowers, and a checkered floor under a large hanging light.](https://hermes.media.static.aol.com/media/2026/06/29/6cc2a8e0-2d63-37f7-8766-9fef12f3bc24/1034c8fe-494c-465f-8591-b066fe8ab571.jpg)

Design by Bethany Adams.JL Jordan Photography

Narrow beadboard paneling is replacing shiplap indoors, especially in homes attempting a cozy, cottage vibe.

“Adding a beadboard ceiling to rooms, which often have a different ceiling elevation than the rest of the space, draws attention to the quirkiness in the best possible way,” Adams says. “Beadboard ceilings have historically been present on porches and sunrooms, so by incorporating that material in an enclosed portion of the home, you're hinting at summer days spent sipping iced tea, even if you're just sipping coffee at your breakfast table.”

## Painted Trellis

![Elegant room with decorative lattice walls, arched window, marble table, and a vintage bar cart with glassware and bottles.](https://hermes.media.static.aol.com/media/2026/06/29/6cc2a8e0-2d63-37f7-8766-9fef12f3bc24/16f40536-8078-412b-b1ac-3a62e4f50481.jpeg)

Design byHouse of Honey.Michael P. H. Clifford

For a design element rooted in tradition, trelliswork feels surprisingly fresh when viewed through a modern lens.

“Trelliswork has a tendency to be pigeonholed as something quaint or overly traditional, which is exactly why we liked the idea of rethinking it,” says Kaye-Honey. To avoid the classic greenhouse aesthetic, she painted the installation a deep, inky blue, giving it a moodier, more contemporary feel. “We treated it almost like paneling, using pattern, shadow, and repetition to create depth and drama.”

## Glass Blocks

![Modern bathroom with green tile walls, glass shower, and white countertop with a vase of flowers.](https://hermes.media.static.aol.com/media/2026/06/29/6cc2a8e0-2d63-37f7-8766-9fef12f3bc24/59290e93-0948-425d-8afc-cfb7993b91d8.jpeg)

Design byDunham Robinson.Nicholas Venezia

Admit it: modern windows can be a little boring. That's one reason designers like Robinson are taking a cue from the 1980s with the return of glass block windows.

Originally used in commercial and industrial buildings to bring natural light into factories, basements, and other dim interiors, these gem-like blocks became defining features of Art Deco and Art Moderne architecture before making their way into homes. They enjoyed a major resurgence in the 1980s, and now they're back once again. Today, designers are embracing glass block windows as sculptural (and practical) design elements.

Advertisement

“Glass block windows serve the practical purpose of bringing in daylight without compromising privacy,” says Robinson, who used the 8-by-8-inch blocks as an enlarged counterpart to the 4-by-4-inch wall tiles. “Their subtle green tint reinforces the room's tonal palette.”

Those in the market for pieces with richer textures and patterns, call up salvage yards and architectural reuse centers, which can carry affordable inventory.

## Tapestries

![Cozy living room featuring an arched entrance, plush seating, a decorative tapestry, and natural light from large windows.](https://hermes.media.static.aol.com/media/2026/06/29/6cc2a8e0-2d63-37f7-8766-9fef12f3bc24/6a7bb678-9c99-452d-add6-097354cfa152.jpeg)

Design byShane & Pierce.Michael P.H. Clifford

The antidote to sterile and beige interiors of the past decade is to pluck a piece from the past—ideally, one that has a story. To add extra depth to a room treated with Venetian plaster, Pierce Jordan of [Shane & Pierce](https://www.shaneandpierce.com/) in California and Texas used a tapestry as a medium that felt richer than traditional art.

“I think tapestries have made a massive comeback,” Jordan says. “There are so many various versions, and many have multicolor but remain subdued.” By their very nature, tapestries are threaded with character and narrative. This project used a piece that was purchased in Paris in the 1970s and passed down by the owner's grandmother. “Tapestries are an amazing conversation starter because they are generally old, and people want to know the story behind them,” he adds.

## Vintage 4-by-4-Inch Tile

![joshjoshgrubbsphotocom](https://hermes.media.static.aol.com/media/2026/06/29/6cc2a8e0-2d63-37f7-8766-9fef12f3bc24/32fd174e-01c6-4f11-a64f-ce66e271fc29.jpg)

Design byHeather Peterson Design.JOSH GRUBBS

Subway tile is certainly on its way out, and 4-by-4-inch ceramic tile is back in. It was a top pick for the post-WWII housing boom through the 1970s. Many historical homes still have the cheeky squares in pastels, often with contrasting trim pieces. Designers today are embracing the look and even seeking out original vintage tiles. [Heather Peterson](https://www.heatherpetersondesign.com/), for one, sourced Talavera tile on Etsy for a bar under the stairs. The pattern was used to “dress up the whole corner” in a single-row application.

Robinson seeks out ceramic tile from defunct American manufacturers by perusing [Vintage Tile Shop](https://vintagetileshop.com/), a resource for original designs that are actually new rather than salvaged.

“Many older American tile manufacturers produced extraordinarily nuanced glazes and colors that are difficult to replicate today,” she says. “These materials remain relatively affordable because they're often viewed as salvage rather than collectible design objects, but that perception is beginning to change.”

## Fluted Glass

![Modern kitchen featuring wooden cabinetry, white drawers, and a checkered black and white floor with natural light.](https://hermes.media.static.aol.com/media/2026/06/29/6cc2a8e0-2d63-37f7-8766-9fef12f3bc24/fa63a474-f5f4-483a-a940-421cc92cd5ef.jpeg)

Design by Jeanne Hayes, founder of Camden Grace Interiors.Jared Kuzia

Fluted glass may be known from the Art Deco and Victorian eras, but it has a rich history that goes back to the Egyptian pharaohs. This glass is also called “reeded” or “pressed” glass, with early versions made of molten glass that was molded into the ripple shape.

Jeanne Hayes, founder of [Camden Grace Interiors](http://www.camden-grace.com/) in West Hartford, Connecticut, uses it to obscure “the realities of the day-to-day,” while giving a space an almost enigmatic appeal.

## Open Millwork

![Modern interior featuring a staircase, dining area, and stylish furnishings with a vibrant color scheme.](https://hermes.media.static.aol.com/media/2026/06/29/6cc2a8e0-2d63-37f7-8766-9fef12f3bc24/a603c8c0-a819-4956-98c1-747aa2bb1788.jpeg)

Design byLaura W. Jenkins.Joseph Bradshaw

Jenkins is seeing a resurgence in feature fireplaces and open millwork—both of which offer a nod to midcentury architecture and act as eye-catching architectural elements. Unlike a slatted room divider that functions to compartmentalize the space, the millwork works double duty to bring in storage, which is something most families need in contemporary designs. Unlike the reddish woods that were prevalent in midcentury and 1990s looks, the current interpretations lean closer to white oaks and birch.

## Inglenooks

![Cozy living space featuring framed art, a red armchair, and an inviting green window seat with cushions and plants.](https://hermes.media.static.aol.com/media/2026/06/29/6cc2a8e0-2d63-37f7-8766-9fef12f3bc24/bbdc5703-343f-492a-8400-a14734a783c2.jpg)

Design by Tom Riker and James Dolenc ofJames Thomas.Nick Johnson

Midcentury modern certainly has its place, but designers are looking as far back [as the Middle Ages](https://www.housebeautiful.com/design-inspiration/a71362909/medieval-interiors-trend-2026/) for inspiration.

“Inglenooks are having a resurgence as homeowners seek cozy spaces within larger homes,” says Tom Riker, partner at [James Thomas](https://jamesthomasinteriors.com/) in Chicago. The design studio repurposed an 18th-century French limestone mantel and surround to create a dramatic inglenook, lining the interior with walnut paneling and creating an inviting spot for late-night cocktails.

## Arched French Doors

![Modern living room featuring comfortable seating, wood furnishings, plants, and decorative artifacts.](https://hermes.media.static.aol.com/media/2026/06/29/6cc2a8e0-2d63-37f7-8766-9fef12f3bc24/e41fb292-0dea-4e56-828e-9a045dd96974.jpeg)

Design byShannon Eddings Interiors.Molly Culver

Arched glass entryways are symbolic of the Italian Renaissance—it was the go-to style used in the Florentine Medici residences. The classic Roman arches defined the semicircular fanlights that were then prevalent in Georgian and Federal architecture, and are looking quite at home in modern interiors.

Designer Shannon Eddings left the original doors of this 1922 home to give the living space and dining room a sense of symmetry.

## Original Millwork

![Dimly lit dining room featuring a long table with upholstered chairs and a contemporary light fixture.](https://hermes.media.static.aol.com/media/2026/06/29/6cc2a8e0-2d63-37f7-8766-9fef12f3bc24/f99b83f2-fccb-4e1e-ac30-5a0fb395b77a.jpeg)

Design by Hayley Chavez ofHLC Studio.Ellie Koleen

For Hayley Chavez of [HLC Studio](https://www.hlcstudio.com/) in Clovis, California, it’s all about embracing the original architectural details—leaning into warm wood tones, built-ins, and rough-hewn beams—instead of starting fresh.

“We love working with original millwork, as it brings immediate depth and character to a room,” says Chavez. “Whether it’s original floors, picture rails, or cabinetry, classic details bring soul to a home.” A picture rail helps carry your eye around the space, and the exposed beams on a Craftsman ceiling create architectural transparency rooted in authenticity.

To highlight both, Chavez brought the walls back to plaster with Roman Clay by [Portola Paints](https://portolapaints.com/) to give the house that “original worn look” that could never be achieved with flat drywall.

**Follow** _**House Beautiful**_ **on** [**Instagram**](https://www.instagram.com/housebeautiful/) **and** [**TikTok**](https://go.redirectingat.com/?id=74968X1788647&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.tiktok.com%2F%40housebeautiful%3Flang%3Den&sref=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.housebeautiful.com%2Fdesign-inspiration%2Fa71770154%2Fvintage-design-details-modern-interiors%2F) **.**

San Diego, CA











0



1



0















































































Nov 14, 2020













First to Review







We bought a fixer-upper in Indialantic and wanted to do a large scale renovation, all new floors, update the bathrooms, fresh paint and more. We met with two other GCs prior to meeting with Dan Crawford of CCI, but Dan impressed us with his timeliness and professionalism. This was our first home (and first renovation), and while we had a good idea of what we wanted, we needed help working through the steps of getting there. Dan provided good ideas for improving our plans, and he worked with us through the entire three month renovation cycle (ended on schedule). As problems came up, Dan worked with us to quickly resolve them. We've been in the house for a couple of months now, and the place looks amazing. It's undergone a complete transformation from its old 1990's style to looking more like a magazine showroom. It's apparent that Dan and his crew take pride in their work, and we're extremely satisfied choosing CCI for our project. I know choosing a GC can be tough, but I highly recommend Dan and CCI!

metadata.ogDescription:
Established in 1998.  At CCI Construction of Brevard, Inc., a Commercial and Residential Construction Company, we believe remodeling and new construction should be a pleasant experience. Call us if in need of a General Contractor.

We will do everything possible to see that your construction project flows smoothly and is completed in a timely matter..

Our mission has remained constant; deliver high-quality, cost-effective construction projects on schedule which meet our clients needs and exceed their expectations.

We also have wide ranging experience with commercial projects, including retail stores, food service, office interiors and warehouse construction. Having completed many such projects, we understand the essentials of giving our customer the best value for their investment and turning over a completed project as quickly as possible.

--- | | | | | | --- | --- | | ![Industry News](https://www.lightdirectory.com/images/industry_news_grey.png) | | LEDucation 2027 Opens Call for Speakers for Expanded Three-Day Conference
June 30, 2026
LEDucation 2027 is now accepting speaker proposals for its expanded educational program. The conference will feature virtual sessions on Thursday, April 8, and Friday, April 9, 2027, followed by in-person presentations during the three-day LEDucation event, April 13–15, 2027 at the New York Hilton Midtown, New York City. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-LEDucation-2027-Opens-Call-for-Speakers-for-Expanded-Three-Day-Conference.htm)
Alloy LED Partners with International Lights to Expand Representation in New York and New Jersey
June 25, 2026
Alloy LED, a leading designer and manufacturer of premium LED tape lighting solutions, announced a new partnership with International Lights, a respected lighting agency serving the New York metropolitan region. Through the partnership, International Lights will represent and support Alloy LED across metropolitan New York, Long Island and northern New Jersey territories. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-Alloy-LED-Partners-with-International-Lights-to-Expand-Representation-in-New-York-and-New-Jersey.htm)
Architecture Firm Billings Weaken Further as Economic Uncertainty Slows Projects
June 24, 2026
While growth appeared to be approaching earlier this year, the current economic certainty has contributed to business conditions softening across all regions. Billings remained closest to growth at firms located in the South, following the trend for most of the year thus far. Firms of all specializations also reported declining billings this month, after firms with a multifamily residential specialization saw flat to modestly increasing billings in March and April. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-Architecture-Firm-Billings-Weaken-Further-as-Economic-Uncertainty-Slows-Projects.htm)
Eureka Introduces River Luminaire Family
June 24, 2026
Eureka® today announced the release of its River luminaires. The family features a range of pendant and surface-mount fixtures in multiple shapes. River is made with a transparent 3D-printed polymer that creates a signature ridged body. Light and shadow naturally play with the textured surface, creating a distinctive effect. Inside, an LED tube provides continuous illumination of up to 400 lumens per foot. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-Eureka-Introduces-River-Luminaire-Family.htm)
Inter-lux Appoints Linda Carrillo as Market Manager for Western Region
June 23, 2026
As part of its ongoing commitment to delivering exceptional customer support and strengthening market presence, Inter-lux is pleased to announce the appointment of Linda Carrillo as Market Manager for its Western Region. Carrillo will serve as the primary liaison between Inter-lux and its West Coast representative agencies. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-Inter-lux-Appoints-Linda-Carrillo-as-Market-Manager-for-Western-Region.htm)
The Besal Fund Elects Michael Linck as Board President
June 22, 2026
Acuity Brands Lighting (ABL) announced today that Michael Linck, Vice President – Specification Sales, ABL has been elected President of the Besal Fund Board. The Robert J. Besal Memorial Education Fund (Besal Fund), is a trust established to provide a continuing scholarship program for the advancement of education in illuminating engineering disciplines. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-The-Besal-Fund-Elects-Michael-Linck-as-Board-President.htm)
Canada Light Expo 2026 To Unite the Canadian Lighting Industry Under One Roof
June 17, 2026
Canada Light Expo 2026, the country’s premier exhibition for the lighting and LED technology sector, will return to the International Centre, Toronto, on 18–19 November 2026. Marking its 3rd edition, the event is expected to bring together more than 100 leading brands and showcase over 1,000 lighting solutions, connecting manufacturers, designers, specifiers, distributors, contractors, and decision-makers from across the global lighting industry. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-Canada-Light-Expo-2026-To-Unite-the-Canadian-Lighting-Industry-Under-One-Roof.htm)
Eureka Lighting Wins Best of the Best Red Dot Award for Segment
June 11, 2026
Eureka, an established leader in decorative lighting solutions, is pleased to announce that its Segment luminaire family was awarded the Red Dot: Best of the Best distinction in the 2026 Product Design competition. This is the second time that Eureka has received this elevated award status; it won “Best of the Best” for Tangram-Trace in 2023. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-Eureka-Lighting-Wins-Best-of-the-Best-Red-Dot-Award-for-Segment.htm)
Luminis Wins Two Red Dot Awards
June 11, 2026
Luminis, an established innovator and manufacturer of specification-grade, interior and exterior lighting solutions, today announced its Ellington and Trilo families have each received a Red Dot Design Award for Product Design. Dating back to 1955, the Red Dot Design Award is one of the world’s largest design competitions. Every year, the Red Dot Award: Product Design sets out to find the year’s best products. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-Luminis-Wins-Two-Red-Dot-Awards.htm)
ArchLIGHT Summit 2026 Details Must-Attend Sessions
June 10, 2026
Dallas Market Center’s ArchLIGHT Summit, the growing annual lighting trade and educational event for the architectural, specification, and design communities, has opened attendee registration for its September 15-16, 2026 show and announced event highlights. This year’s event welcomes prominent thought leaders to deliver targeted education via panels and discussions for professionals in lighting design, architecture, specification, and interior design. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-ArchLIGHT-Summit-2026-Details-Must-Attend-Sessions.htm)
May 2026 NEMA Electroindustry Business Confidence Index
June 8, 2026
The Current Conditions component registered 47.7 in May, a decline of 2.3 percentage points from April's reading of 50.0. The prior month’s result suggested only a pause from March’s decline to 44.0, with no sustained recovery having taken hold as May’s reading marked a return to contraction. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-May-2026-NEMA-Electroindustry-Business-Confidence-Index-.htm)
ILLI Fall 2026 Intensive Course
June 8, 2026
After a successful Course in Virginia Beach, ILLI returns to El Monte Sagrado in Taos, New Mexico for the Fall 2026 Intensive Course, five days of immersive learning, hands-on field work, and mentorship from some of the industry's most respected professionals. While the Spring 2025 Intensive Course attendees created amazing display areas, we felt we barely touched on the opportunities for lighting design that are at El Monte Sagrado. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-ILLI-Fall-2026-Intensive-Course.htm)
Alloy LED and Optique Lighting Strengthen Midwest Market Presence Through PG enlighten Partnership
June 4, 2026
Alloy LED, a leading designer and manufacturer of premium LED tape lighting solutions, and its specification division, Optique Lighting, renowned for its high-performance Nano Linear LED luminaires, are pleased to announce a new partnership with PG enlighten. Through the partnership, PG enlighten will represent and support the companies’ lighting solutions across the Illinois and Northwest Indiana markets. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-Alloy-LED-and-Optique-Lighting-Strengthen-Midwest-Market-Presence-Through-PG-enlighten-Partnership.htm)
Dallas Market Center Opens Registration for ArchLIGHT Summit 2026 and Announces New Advisory Board
June 3, 2026
Dallas Market Center has opened attendee registration for the 2026 ArchLIGHT Summit, taking place September 15–16, while also announcing the addition of three prominent industry leaders to the event’s Advisory Board. Together, these updates underscore the continued growth and influence of the annual lighting trade and educational event for the architectural, specification, and design communities. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-Dallas-Market-Center-Opens-Registration-for-ArchLIGHT-Summit-2026-and-Announces-New-Advisory-Board.htm)
Solais Lighting Appoints Al Near as Chief Commercial Officer
June 1, 2026
Near joins Solais after more than 30 years in the lighting and controls industry, where he has held leadership roles across manufacturing, sales, channel management, acquisitions, and business development. He will lead the company’s commercial sales organization and work alongside Solais leadership to continue expanding the brand’s presence across North America. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-Solais-Lighting-Appoints-Al-Near-as-Chief-Commercial-Officer.htm)
Eureka Expands Segment Luminaire Family
May 28, 2026
Eureka announces the expansion of its award winning Segment luminaire family. The slim pendant is now offered in a shorter length and in three new preconfigured geometric forms. Previously available in four , six , and eight foot lengths, Segment is now also offered in a sleek two foot option. In addition, specifiers may select square, rectangular, or hexagonal configurations, with side lengths ranging from two to eight feet.
[Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-Eureka-Expands-Segment-Luminaire-Family.htm)
The Lighting Quotient Announces tambient ona at Fulton Market Design Days 2026
May 28, 2026
The Lighting Quotient (LiQ) announces tambient® ona™, its new human-centric workplace lighting system, now in full production. The system will make its public debut at Fulton Market Design Days in Chicago, June 8 –10, 2026. Built for today’s dynamic workplaces, tambient® ona™, offers unmatched flexibility. Its wireless, mobile-commissioned control system allows lighting to adapt as spaces evolve whether reconfiguring layouts, relocating offices or retrofitting existing buildings. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-The-Lighting-Quotient-Announces-tambient-ona-at-Fulton-Market-Design-Days-2026.htm)
NEMA Launches National Campaign Spotlighting the Essential Role of American Electrical Manufacturers
May 26, 2026
NEMA recently launched a new national advertising campaign raising awareness of the growing role America’s electrical manufacturers play in powering our grid, expanding U.S. manufacturing, and electrifying our homes and transportation systems. The campaign will run across digital and streaming platforms and national news outlets, emphasizing the electroindustry’s role in strengthening U.S. supply chains, supporting reindustrialization, and building the infrastructure needed to meet rising electricity demand. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-NEMA-Launches-National-Campaign-Spotlighting-the-Essential-Role-of-American-Electrical-Manufacturers.htm)
AIA Deltek ABI - Billing Activity at Architecture Firms Moderates
May 20, 2026
Architecture firm billings declined modestly in April as broader economic instability continued. The AIA/Deltek Architecture Billings Index® (ABI) fell from 49.8 in March, when billings were essentially flat, to 48.3 in April, signaling that more firms reported declining billings than rising billings. National architecture firm billings have remained below the 50-point growth threshold since January 2023. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-AIA-Deltek-ABI---Billing-Activity-at-Architecture-Firms-Moderates.htm)
Lighting Controls Podcast - Technology Doesnt Have to be Scary with Jonathan Perlis
May 14, 2026
In this episode, we dive into the real transition happening in lighting—moving from simply selling fixtures to delivering full lighting control solutions. We also explore unique challenges in the Canadian market—from 347V systems to code differences—and why standardizing controls design could change everything. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-Lighting-Controls-Podcast---Technology-Doesnt-Have-to-be-Scary-with-Jonathan-Perlis.htm)
April 2026 NEMA Electroindustry Business Confidence Index
May 11, 2026
The Current Conditions component increased to 50.0, rising from 44.0 last month and moving to the expansionary threshold. Twenty-seven percent of respondents reported “better” conditions, 27 percent reported “worse,” and 45 percent indicated conditions were unchanged. Compared to the prior month, the distribution of responses suggested improved momentum, with a larger share reporting gains and a smaller share reporting deterioration. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-April-2026-NEMA-Electroindustry-Business-Confidence-Index.htm)
New NEMA Study Shows Widening Gap Between Surging Power Demand and Grid Capacity
May 7, 2026
he National Electrical Manufacturers Association (NEMA) today released an updated edition of its landmark A Reliable Grid for an Electric Future study along with a suite of new reports outlining the promise and potential of Grid-Enhancing Technologies (GETs), Behind-the-Meter (BTM) resources, and Demand Response to meet intensifying grid reliability, resilience, and affordability challenges – as well as the policy measures needed to deploy these solutions at scale. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-New-NEMA-Study-Shows-Widening-Gap-Between-Surging-Power-Demand-and-Grid-Capacity.htm)
Radialux 5.5 COB Combines RGB Color-Changing with Tunable White
May 6, 2026
Alloy LED’s Radialux 5.5 COB RGBTW Tape Light represents a refined convergence of performance and design versatility. This premium five-channel tape light seamlessly integrates dynamic RGB color-changing with tunable white light ranging from 2700K to 6500K, all within a dot-free Chip-on-Board (COB) construction. The result is a smooth, uninterrupted line of illumination that elevates architectural spaces. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-Radialux-5.5-COB-Combines-RGB-Color-Changing-with-Tunable-White.htm)
DOE Publishes Second Round of Germicidal Ultraviolet Product Test Results
May 4, 2026
The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) has published the second round of CALiPER test results for germicidal ultraviolet (GUV) products. These Round 2 results further demonstrate the importance of education and industry standards in the GUV product industry. GUV air treatment is an effective and energy-efficient method to reduce airborne disease transmission in combination with ventilation and filtration. GUV can play a critical role in meeting advanced indoor air quality standards using less energy. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-DOE-Publishes-Second-Round-of-Germicidal-Ultraviolet-Product-Test-Results-.htm)
Dallas Market Center to Host 3rd Annual DALI Summit Alongside ArchLIGHT Summit in September
April 30, 2026
Dallas Market Center announces that the 3rd Annual DALI Summit, hosted by the DALI Alliance, will take place September 16 and 17 alongside ArchLIGHT Summit, the commercial and architectural lighting event held September 15 and 16. Co-locating the DALI Summit with ArchLIGHT Summit allows attendees to explore both advanced lighting control technologies and design-focused programming within a single visit. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-Dallas-Market-Center-to-Host-3rd-Annual-DALI-Summit-Alongside-ArchLIGHT-Summit-in-September.htm)
2026 Featured LEDucation Products from Light Directory
April 27, 2026
Welcome to the 7th annual Featured LEDucation Products from Light Directory!! Please check out some of the products that were featured at the LEDucation show. Use the form at bottom of the page to request additional information about any of the products featured. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-2026-Featured-LEDucation-Products-from-Light-Directory.htm)
ABI Signaling Market Stabilization with Near Flat Billing Activity
April 22, 2026
Architecture firm billings held steady in March, indicating market stability despite global economic concerns. The AIA/Deltek Architecture Billings Index (ABI) scored 49.8, reflecting near-equal shares of firms reporting increases and decreases. This marks the closest the index has come to the 50-point growth threshold since early 2023, offering cautious optimism.
[Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-ABI-Signaling-Market-Stabilization-with-Near-Flat-Billing-Activity.htm)
Velino.0 Brings High-End Performance While Virtually Disappearing into the Architecture
April 21, 2026
As the slimmest, low UGR architectural luminaires on the market, the Velino.0 family is engineered for total design freedom, seamless integration and with advanced optical performance. Featuring a sleek ½-inch aperture, the advanced optics include a curated portfolio of professional-grade optical solutions including high-performance glare control, ergonomic distribution, asymmetric optics and dark-optics technology, alongside value-driven diffuser options. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-Velino.0-Brings-High-End-Performance-While-Virtually-Disappearing-into-the-Architecture.htm)
LEDucation 2026 Marks 20 Years of Growth, Community, and Industry Impact
April 20, 2026
The LEDucation Trade Show and Conference, organized by the Designers Lighting Forum of New York (DLFNY), concluded another successful event at the New York Hilton Midtown on April 14–15, 2026. Now celebrating its 20th anniversary, LEDucation welcomed over 10,000 attendees and 565 exhibitors from around the globe, including a strong presence of new exhibitors, reinforcing its position as North America’s largest annual lighting industry gathering. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-LEDucation-2026-Marks-20-Years-of-Growth%2C-Community%2C-and-Industry-Impact.htm)
Selux Line Family Expansion
April 13, 2026
Selux expands the Line Family with the addition of Line Wall and Line Pure—bringing together a cohesive architectural lighting offering defined by clarity, precision, and minimalist form. Together, the Line Collection provides designers with a unified aesthetic across vertical surfaces and open landscapes—combining high-performance optics, visual comfort, and durable construction for long-term exterior performance. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-Selux-Line-Family-Expansion.htm)
Olivio Grande with Gen5 Optics and Olivio Medio with RGBW Expand Technical Capabilities
April 13, 2026
New performance-driven enhancements to the Olivio outdoor luminaire family introduce advanced optical control in Olivio Grande and dynamic color capability in Olivio Medio. Olivio Grande is now equipped with the proprietary Gen5 silicone optic, a significant optical advancement engineered to deliver a full range of precise light distributions with exceptional visual comfort. [Read more](https://www.lightdirectory.com/news-Olivio-Grande-with-Gen5-Optics-and-Olivio-Medio-with-RGBW-Expand-Technical-Capabilities-.htm)
Lighting Controls Podcast - Be Disruptive with Bolis Ibrahim

[Brenton B.](https://www.yelp.com/user_details?userid=rFRMX9m7VxO2_pAtg71SVg)

San Diego, CA











0



1



0















































































Nov 14, 2020













First to Review







We bought a fixer-upper in Indialantic and wanted to do a large scale renovation, all new floors, update the bathrooms, fresh paint and more. We met with two other GCs prior to meeting with Dan Crawford of CCI, but Dan impressed us with his timeliness and professionalism. This was our first home (and first renovation), and while we had a good idea of what we wanted, we needed help working through the steps of getting there. Dan provided good ideas for improving our plans, and he worked with us through the entire three month renovation cycle (ended on schedule). As problems came up, Dan worked with us to quickly resolve them. We've been in the house for a couple of months now, and the place looks amazing. It's undergone a complete transformation from its old 1990's style to looking more like a magazine showroom. It's apparent that Dan and his crew take pride in their work, and we're extremely satisfied choosing CCI for our project. I know choosing a GC can be tough, but I highly recommend Dan and CCI!

metadata.ogDescription:
Established in 1998.  At CCI Construction of Brevard, Inc., a Commercial and Residential Construction Company, we believe remodeling and new construction should be a pleasant experience. Call us if in need of a General Contractor.

We will do everything possible to see that your construction project flows smoothly and is completed in a timely matter..

Our mission has remained constant; deliver high-quality, cost-effective construction projects on schedule which meet our clients needs and exceed their expectations.

We also have wide ranging experience with commercial projects, including retail stores, food service, office interiors and warehouse construction. Having completed many such projects, we understand the essentials of giving our customer the best value for their investment and turning over a completed project as quickly as possible.

A History and Biographical Cyclopaedia of Butler County, Ohio Page 1 of 1

William WEBSTER was born in Liberty Township on the 2d of May, 1811, and is the son of William
WEBSTER and Mary MARSH, who came to this county from New Jersey in 1806. Mr. WEBSTER was
early in life engaged in the hardware business in Hamilton, in conjunction with his brother, Joseph
WEBSTER, but for the last forty years has been a resident of Middletown. He has been twice married.
His first wife was Maria J. KENNEDY, and he was married to her October 5, 1837. Her father's name
was Joel KENNEDY, and her mother's Esther MOOREHOUSE. By her he had eight children. Albert
WEBSTER was born September 26, 1838, and died on the 5th of December, 1851; Mary was born April
12, 1840, and died February 7, 1841; Joel K. was born May 29, 1843, and now lives in Kentucky; Laura
J. FORD was born December 3, 1845, and lives in Texas; Charles was born November 30, 1848, and
died June 27, 1868; Florence NUNNELLY was born July 29, 1852, and lives in Galveston, Texas;
Thomas was born August 15, 1854, and William E. was born October 2, 1856. In a second marriage,
Mr. WEBSTER was untied to Charlotte E. HOOK on the 6th of August, 1869, who has one child,
Stanley, born September 30, 1876.

Mary,\
\
606 N. 9th is our home! And yes, it is still beautiful. We have been restoring\
it for the last 3 years. We have torn off layers and layers of wallpaper and\
repaired and replastered it from ceiling to floor boards to it's original glory.\
We were able to restore the original entryway floor. We also put a pool and\
outdoor fireplace in which was designed to compliment the period of the\
home. Our next project is a new entryway system.\
\
We moved to Temple in 1993 from (ironically) Indiana. We moved to San\
Diego in 2001 and back in 2004. That's when we purchased 606 N. 9th. My\
husband, William, and our 3 sons love it here. We had always admired it for\
years. | am currently researching the history of this magnificent home so\
that | can get a much deserved Texas historic marker for it.\
\
| spent 4 hours in the library last night. With the help of a Temple historian,\
we were able to track all the owners of our house. The records are very\
difficult to research back that far. | have yet to go to the county offices for\
deed and tax records.\
\
| have a few questions....\
1. From what | can tell so far, Richard Foord looks to have built our home. Is\
that correct??? If so, what is the exact year?\
\
2. Did Richard, Laura, Jackson, and Florence all live together here at the\
same time??? The city directories indicated that.\
\
3. In 1939, a W. E. Webster resided here. Is he related to Florence Webster\
Nunneley??\
\
lam so excited about this research. | would LOVE to find old\
Foord/Nunneley family photos and display them in the house. | have\
enclosed a drawing and a recent photo of our home during our historic 4th of\
July parade. We are board members of in the Temple Historic Preservation\
League. We host many neighborhood outings throughout the year on our\
front lawn and pool area.\
\
Jane Hunt\

Jane,\
‘The stone at Hillcrest Cemetery in Temple, Texas is engraved as follows:\
\
LAURA JANE / WIFE OF CAPT.R. H. FOORD / 1845 - 1917\
\
The online posting for Hillcrest Cemetery shows her name to be Laura Jane Nunneley, wife of Cap. R. H.\
Foord. This is an error. Laura Jane was the daughter of William and Mariah Webster. She married Richard\
H. Foord/Ford on 24 Dec 1863 in Butler County Ohio. Laura and Richard moved to Indiana by 1870 and finally\
to Temple, Bell Co. TX. She was the sister of Florence Webster Nunneley, wife of Jackson Baldwin Nunneley.\
| visited Hillcrest Cemetery in Temple several years ago (it is beautiful) and took pictures of the tombstones of\
the Foords and Nunneleys. The posting on the internet for Hillcrest is a great site for historians and\
genealogists. As a member of the White Rose Cemetery Board in Bartlesville, Oklahoma, | must tell you that\
"cleaning up" the records for an old cemetery is a huge job and one in which | am now involved with our\
\
Board. The following are the listings for the Foord and Nunneley stones:\
\
Jackson Baldwin Nunneley / 1839-1907\
THIS IS CORRECT\
\
Florence / Wife of J. B. Nunneley / 1852-1928\
THIS IS CORRECT\
\
Unknown Nunneley / no dates / D.A.R.\
\
THIS IS INCORRECT...IN FRONT OF THE LARGE NUNNELEY MARKER IS A MARKER WITH THE DAR\
SYMBOL AND SAYS "BETTY MARTIN CHAPTER DAR" THIS IS A MEMORIAL FOR THE MEMBERSHIP\
OF FLORENCE WEBSTER NUNNELEY IN DAR\
\
Laura Jane Nunneley / 1845-1917 / wife of Cap. R. H. Foord\
THIS IS INCORRECT. THE STONE ACTUALLY SAYS, LAURA JANE / WIFE OF CAPT. R. H. FOORD /\
1845-1917. SHE WAS NOT A NUNNELEY, BUT A WEBSTER\
\
Capt. R. H. Foord / 1841-1916\
THIS IS CORRECT\
\
Jackson Nunneley was a prominent citizen of Temple. From various sources | found while visiting Temple, he\
was involved in the following:\
\
Owned, with L. M. Chattin, a mercantile business (they built the first brick building in Temple)\
\
Owned paper bag companies in Austin and Galveston\
\
Charter member of |.0.0.F / K of P/ B.P.O.E\
\
Helped organize the Temple Water Works Co.\
\
Director of the Temple First National Bank until his death\
\
Charter member of the Temple Compress Company / Temple Improvement Company / the Electric Light\
Company the Hotel Company / and the Oil Mill Company.\
\
Charter member of the City Cemetery Company (now Hillcrest)\
\
Hope this helps correct your information. If you ever visit Temple, do go to the cemetery and also, you can see\
the lovely home of Jackson and Florence at 606 North 9th. It is well maintained, or was a few years ago.\
\
Mary R M Nunneley\
Bartlesville Oklahoma\
\

Greg,\
\
Saw your posting concerning Sheriff James David Nunneley of Milam Co. TX, born 4 Mar 1837 in\
Carroll Co. AR. | doubt that the initial D stood for David. James D. had a brother David born 1824 in\
Hickman Co. TN, died 1843 in probably Carroll or Franklin Co. AR. James D. Nunneley was the son\
of Joseph Alexander Nunneley (son of Anderson Nunneley and Martha Baldwin) and Nancy Easley\
(dau of Thomas Easley and Ecleo Wade). | have a picture taken by Jackson Baldwin Nunneley, a\
photographer, brother of James D. Nunneley and it is not identified but surely a Nunneley. Contact me\
for further info concerning this family and a possible location to search for a picture in Bell Co. TX.\
Mary R. M. Nunneley\

I believe your Wm. D. Carter is the brother of my gr-grand\
\
mother MALINDA CAROLINE CARTER, Ist wife of LAWSON HARRISON\
\
NUNNELEY of MADISON CO., AR. I show WM. D. "DICK" CARTER as\
\
being b: SEPT 5, 1852 (insteaad of 1851) in MONROE CO., TN.\
\
whose parents were PATTERSON CARTER b: 1816 in MONROE CO., TN.& EDNA\
"EDA/IDA" CLINE b: 1824 in MONROE CO., TN. WM. D.\
\
married ANNIE E. BUSH bef. 1882 in AR.?, he died SEPT 11, 1919 & is buried in LIBERTY\
BAPTIST CHURCH CEM., DRY FORK,\
\
NEWTON (now CARROLL CO.), AR. I have been to that cemetery many times. Syblings of\
WM.D. were JOHN b: DEC 5, 1855 in\
\
MADISON CoO., AR. & d: JAN 23, 1924 in BLUFF SPRINGS near\
KINGSTON,MADISON,AR.,buried UPPER CAMPGROUND CEM.,MADISON CO., AR. (is\
this your JOHN H.?), He married SARAH "SISIE"\
\
GRIMES; MALINDA CAROLINE,b: DEC 9, 1857 in MADISON CO.,AR.\
\
d: 1931 in DINSMORE,NEWTON/CARROLL,AR.,(my gr-grantmother),\
\
she married LAWSON HARRISON NUNNELEY (ID) IN 1873, MADISON CO.,AR.- MALINDA\
is buried at LIBERTY; LAWSON in LeFLORE CO.,OK. with his 2nd wife. JAMES A. "Stringy",\
b:1858 in MADISON CO., AR.(said to have worked on a railroad???); NANCY, b: c.1863; ELIZA\
"ANNIE/AIMIE", b:JAN 13, 1865, d:DEC 7, 1911,married c.1882 JOS.PRICE SISCO, she is\
buried\
\
in UPPER CAMPGROUND CEM.,MADISON CO.,AR.; SAMUEL, b:1868.\
\
I don't know your J. G. CARTER or your JOHN H. CARTER unless\
\
he is in the above but WM. D. is certainly my relative & if\
\
we can work on this together, will be gald to have you or someone else identify them thru what I\
have posted here.\

Date: Fri, 21 Mar 2008 09:39:30 -0700 (PDT)\
From: "Mary R. M. Nunneley" <nunne00O2@sbcglobal.net>\
\
Subject: Re: Jackson B. Nunneley\
\
To: "Jane Hunt" <texasdoors@hot.rr.com>\
CC: mld71124@dukenet.us, Hillcrest_Cemetery @yahoo.com\
Jane,\
\
‘The stone at Hillcrest Cemetery in Temple, Texas is engraved as follows:\
LAURA JANE / WIFE OF CAPT.R. H. FOORD / 1845 - 1917\
\
The online posting for Hillcrest Cemetery shows her name to be Laura Jane Nunneley, wife of Cap. R. H.\
Foord. This is an error. Laura Jane was the daughter of William and Mariah Webster. She married Richard H.\
Foord/Ford on 24 Dec 1863 in Butler County Ohio. Laura and Richard moved to Indiana by 1870 and finally to\
Temple, Bell Co. TX. She was the sister of Florence Webster Nunneley, wife of Jackson Baldwin Nunneley.\
\
| visited Hillcrest Cemetery in Temple several years ago (it is beautiful) and took pictures of the tombstones of\
the Foords and Nunneleys. The posting on the internet for Hillcrest is a great site for historians and\
genealogists. As a member of the White Rose Cemetery Board in Bartlesville, Oklahoma, | must tell you that\
"cleaning up" the records for an old cemetery is a huge job and one in which | am now involved with our Board.\
The following are the listings for the Foord and Nunneley stones:\
\
Jackson Baldwin Nunneley / 1839-1907\
THIS IS CORRECT\
\
Florence / Wife of J. B. Nunneley / 1852-1928\
THIS IS CORRECT\
\
Unknown Nunneley / no dates / D.A.R.\
\
THIS IS INCORRECT...IN FRONT OF THE LARGE NUNNELEY MARKER IS A MARKER WITH THE DAR\
SYMBOL AND SAYS "BETTY MARTIN CHAPTER DAR" THIS IS A MEMORIAL FOR THE MEMBERSHIP OF\
FLORENCE WEBSTER NUNNELEY IN DAR\
\
Laura Jane Nunneley / 1845-1917 / wife of Cap. R. H. Foord\
THIS IS INCORRECT. THE STONE ACTUALLY SAYS, LAURA JANE / WIFE OF CAPT. R. H. FOORD / 1845-\
1917. SHE WAS NOT A NUNNELEY, BUT A WEBSTER\
\
Capt. R. H. Foord / 1841-1916\
THIS IS CORRECT\
\
Jackson Nunneley was a prominent citizen of Temple. From various sources | found while visiting Temple, he\
was involved in the following:\
\
Owned, with L. M. Chattin, a mercantile business (they built the first brick building in Temple)\
\
Owned paper bag companies in Austin and Galveston\
\
Charter member of 1.0.0.F / K of P/ B.P.O.E\
\
Helped organize the Temple Water Works Co.\
\
Director of the Temple First National Bank until his death\
\
Charter member of the Temple Compress Company / Temple Improvement Company / the Electric Light\
Company the Hotel Company / and the Oil Mill Company.\
\
Charter member of the City Cemetery Company (now Hillcrest)\
\
Hope this helps correct your information. If you ever visit Temple, do go to the cemetery and also, you can see\
the lovely home of Jackson and Florence at 606 North 9th. It is well maintained, or was a few years ago.\
\
Mary R M Nunneley\
Bartlesville Oklahoma\

Jane Hunt <texasdoors@hot.rr.com> wrote:\
\
Regarding your below post about Jackson Nunneley: Was Jackson related to Laura J. Nunneley Foord\
from Temple, TX??? | am researching my 100+ home and his name was listed as living here......\
Please help.\
\
Jane\
\
Greg,\
\
Saw your posting concerning Sheriff James David Nunneley of Milam Co. TX, born 4 Mar 1837 in\
Carroll Co. AR. | doubt that the initial D stood for David. James D. had a brother David born 1824 in\
Hickman Co. TN, died 1843 in probably Carroll or Franklin Co. AR. James D. Nunneley was the son of\
Joseph Alexander Nunneley (son of Anderson Nunneley and Martha Baldwin) and Nancy Easley (dau\
of Thomas Easley and Ecleo Wade). | have a picture taken by Jackson Baldwin Nunneley, a\
photographer, brother of James D. Nunneley and it is not identified but surely a Nunneley. Contact me\
for further info concerning this family and a possible location to search for a picture in Bell Co. TX.\
Mary R. M. Nunneley\

TEMPLE (TEXAS) TIMES / FRIDAY - 15 MARCH 1907

(Pages not numbered in this issue of newspaper)

After a short illness with acute pneumonia, J. B. Nunneley, departed
from this life last Monday evening at 7:10 o'clock and was laid to rest
in the City Cemetery Wednesday morning at 10 o'clock. The hearts of all
who knew him are filled with sorrow, for by his gentle, modest and
unassuming manner he made many friends and kept them. Mr. Nunneley was
one of the early citizens of Temple, coming here when lots were first
placed on sale and investing extensively in Temple property. At an
early date, he, in connection with L. M. Chattin, engaged in the general
mercantile business on old 12th St. They built and occupied the first
brick building in Temple, and as their business increased the store was
enlarged to meet the demands. In addition to his mecrantile business
Mc. Nunneley had other interests in large tracts of western lands, and
in paper bag factories located in Austin and Galveston. He was always
closely identified with the city's civil and educational advancement and
was one of the first aldermen of the city, in which office he helped to
frame the laws governing the same. He was a charter member of the
Pero OLeeP. and) BeP.O.E. In the early days of Temple he was a
factor in the organization of the Temple Water Works Co., and, the First
National Bank of which he remained a director to the day of his death.
He was a charter member of the Temple Compress Company, the Temple
Improvement Co., and the City Cemetery Co., the Electric Light Co., the
Hotel Co., and the Oil Mill Co.

Mc. Nunneley was born in Carroll county, Ark., Oct. 8, 1839. In 1870 he
was inarcied to Miss Florence Webster of Chicago and in 1871 he and his
wife came to Texas, locating at Harrisville where Mr. Nunneley engaged
in photography a short while before entering the mercnatile business.
During all the twenty-five years of his residence in Temple, Mr.
Nunneley has taken a keen interest in all her enterprises and he was one
of the first trustees of the Carnegie Library Association, to which he
has made several donations. Temple has lost one of her oldest and best
citizens and as an expression of the high esteem in which he was held
all business was suspended during the funeral hours Wednesday morning.
Funeral srevices were conducted by the Knights of Pythias, with short
and eloquent tributes to his life by Judge W. S. Lemly, and Judge W. S.
Banks. The floral offerings were many, and beautiful. The active pall
bearers were:

Messrs L. M. Chattin, T. L. Hollingsworth, F. F. Downs, P. L. Downs, W.
S. Banks and Geo. E. Willcox. The honorary pall bearers were: W. H.
Stegall, H. L. Sherrill, H. M. McCelvey, C. B. Hutchison, A. P. Bentley
andes P. Hamill

TEMPLE (TEXAS) DAILY TELEGRAM / Saturday - 28 Jan 1928 - Page l\
MRS. J. B. NUNNELEY DIES\
\
Widow Of One Of Early Founders Of City Was Long Prominent In Progress Of\
Temple\
\
Mrs. J. B. Nunneley, prominent in the annals of Bell county for more\
than half a century, and widow of a successful early day business man\
who aided in a large way in Temple's building, died at 2:30 a.m. Friday\
after an illness of several months.\
\
Mr. and Mrs. Nunneley came to Bell county in 1871 and first settled at\
Harrisville, a community that was 10 miles south of the present site of\
Temple. He was a merchant there and from 1873 to 1880 was postmaster\
there, laying the foundation for his future success. Among his business\
partners at old Harrisville were Rev. William Thomas and T. L.\
Hollingsworth. He and Mrs. Nunneley moved to Temple when the town was\
started in 1881 and together built up much success with the growing town\
and a considerable fortune. Mr. Nunneley built the first brick building\
in Temple, was a city councilman for many years, was an aid in\
organising the waterworks and scores of other projects for the building\
of Temple and Temple institutions.\
\
Among the early day organizations bearing his name as a charter member\
were the Temple compress company, the Temple Improvement company, the\
Cemetery company, the Electric Light company, the Hotel company, the Oil\
Mill company and others. He was one of those who organized the First\
National Bank and was a director of it. The pioneer firm of Nunneley\
and Chattin did an annual business of $100,000, a high figure in those\
days.\
\
Mrs. Nunneley was his companion in his success and she also traveled\
with him a great deal in trips over this country and to Europe.\
\
After his death she continued as nearly as possible his forward and\
progressive policies toward the town and community and among the largest\
recent public gifts made to Temple was the gift of Mrs. Nunneley to the\
Y.M.C.A. of a fine tile swimming pool.\
\
Her maiden name was Miss Florence Webster and she was born, July 29,\
1852, in Middletown, Ohio, the daughter of William and Jane Webster. Of\
a family of seven, all are dead except one brother, Wm. E. Webster of\
Chicago, Ill., who was here at her bedside when the end came. Other\
celatives here are Miss Helen R. Webster of Chicago, Mrs. Harry Smith,\
of Middleton, Ohio, her two nieces, Adrian N. Webster of Hereford, Tex.,\
a nephew. Mrs. Nunneley was married to Mr. Nunneley at Middletown,\
Ohio, Sept. 3, 1870 and on Christmas day, 1871, they came to Texas,\
settling in Bell county.\
\
Funeral services will be held this afternoon at 3 o'clock at the\
residence, 606 North Ninth street, conducted by Rev. T. Edgar Neal,\
pastor of the First Methodist church. Active pallbearers will be: P. L.\
Downs Sr., J. C. Mitchell, W. S. Rowland, G. E. McCelvey, R. O. Culp,\
James Paton.\
\
Honorary pallbearers will be: Dr. L. W. Pollok, A. C. Buchanan, H. M.\
McCelvey, W. O. Cox, G. E. Byars, J. B. Gregory, Dr. T. F. Bunkley, Dr.\
P. H. Brown, E. B. Greathouse, F. L. Denison, A. J. Jarrell, John A.\
Cole, J. A. Fletcher, J. W. Mosley, J. B. Marshall, T. H. Heard, Egg\
Maples, Joe Hollingsworth, L. M. Chattin, Sam H. Cater and W. R. Butler.\

TEMPLE (TEXAS) DAILY TELEGRAM / Saturday - 28 Jan 1928 - Page 1\
MRS. J. B. NUNNELEY DIES\
\
Widow Of One Of Early Founders Of City Was Long Prominent In Progress Of\
Temple\
\
Mrs. J. B. Nunneley, prominent in the annals of Bell county for more\
than half a century, and widow of a successful early day business man\
who aided in a large way in Temple's building, died at 2:30 a.m. Friday\
after an illness of several months.\
\
Mc. and Mrs. Nunneley came to Bell county in 1871 and first settled at\
Harrisville, a community that was 10 miles south of the present site of\
Temple. He was a merchant there and from 1873 to 1880 was postmaster\
there, laying the foundation for his future success. Among his business\
partners at old Harrisville were Rev. William Thomas and T. L.\
Hollingsworth. He and Mrs. Nunneley moved to Temple when the town was\
started in 1881 and together built up much success with the growing town\
and a considerable fortune. Mr. Nunneley built the first brick building\
in Temple, was a city councilman for many years, was an aid in\
organising the waterworks and scores of other projects for the building\
of Temple and Temple institutions.\
\
Among the early day organizations bearing his name as a charter member\
were the Temple compress company, the Temple Improvement company, the\
Cemetery company, the Electric Light company, the Hotel company, the Oil\
Mill company and others. He was one of those who organized the First\
National Bank and was a director of it. The pioneer firm of Nunneley\
and Chattin did an annual business of $100,000, a high figure in those\
days.\
\
Mrs. Nunneley was his companion in his success and she also traveled\
with him a great deal in trips over this country and to Europe.\
\
After his death she continued as nearly as possible his forward and\
progressive policies toward the town and community and among the largest\
recent public gifts made to Temple was the gift of Mrs. Nunneley to the\
Y.M.C.A. of a fine tile swimming pool.\
\
Her maiden name was Miss Florence Webster and she was born, July 29,\
1852, in Middletown, Ohio, the daughter of William and Jane Webster. Of\
a family of seven, all are dead except one brother, Wm. E. Webster of\
Chicago, Ill., who was here at her bedside when the end came. Other\
relatives here are Miss Helen R. Webster of Chicago, Mrs. Harry Smith,\
of Middleton, Ohio, her two nieces, Adrian N. Webster of Hereford, Tex.,\
a nephew. Mrs. Nunneley was married to Mr. Nunneley at Middletown,\
Ohio, Sept. 3, 1870 and on Christmas day, 1871, they came to Texas,\
settling in Bell county.\
\
Funeral services will be held this afternoon at 3 o'clock at the\
residence, 606 North Ninth street, conducted by Rev. T. Edgar Neal,\
pastor of the First Methodist church. Active pallbearers will be: P. L.\
Downs Sr.,; J. C. Mitchell, W. S. Rowland, G. E. McCelvey, R. O. Culp,\
James Paton.\
\
Honorary pallbearers will be: Dr. L. W. Pollok, A. C. Buchanan, H. M.\
McCelvey, W. O. Cox, G. E. Byars, J. B. Gregory, Dr. T. F. Bunkley, Dr.\
P. H. Brown, E. B. Greathouse, F. L. Denison, A. J. Jarrell, John A.\
Cole, J. A. Fletcher, J. W. Mosley, J. B. Marshall, T. H. Heard, Egg\
Maples, Joe Hollingsworth, L. M. Chattin, Sam H. Cater and W. R. Butler.\

FRIDAY -— 15 MARCH 1907

LOCAL AND PERSONAL —- COLUMN TWO

Mr. and Mrs. Sam Nunneley of Ft. Smith, Ark., came Tuesday to attend the
funeral of their uncle, J. B. Nunneley, and will remain in the city for

a few days with relatives.

The Nunnallees moved to Bell County in 1884 and farmed
in or near Holland and Salado the rest of their lives. He
died February 18, 1935, and Mary Jane died May 16, 1954.
Their marked graves are in Holland Cemetery.

The seven Nunnallee children were:

1. Mortie Agusta (1880-1955) married Bertha Cald-
well and had three sons: Millard, Ellis (1910-1984) and
Hubert Price. They are buried in Holland Cemetery.

2. Ovie Buford (1885-1970) married Ernest Caldwell
and had four sons: Earl Weldon (1905-1981), Clyde, Lee
Young and Ray Dell, and one daughter, Melba Ruth Kuzel
They are buried in Wilson Valley Cemetery.

3. Edgar (1887-1952) married Pearl Joiner and they
had four children: James Merle (1911-1957), J.B., Rachel
Nunnallee Cryer and Erma Lee Nunnallee Elliott. They are
buried in Holland Cemetery.

4. Hubert Lee (1890-1946) married Erma Moore and
they had two sons: Hue and Bert (1918-1978). Hubert Lee
‘and Bert are buried in the Holland Cemetery.

5. Winnie Marie (b. 1892) married James Elihu Swan
and had three children: Alice Swan Marshall, James Elihu
and Raymon Bobby.

6. Milton Thomas (1895-1940) marned Gladys Turner.
They had four children: Milton Thomas II, Roger, Mary
Helen Nunnallee Shelley and Ranah Carolyn Nunnallee Det-
wiler. They are buried in Holland Cemetery.

7. Mary Gertie (1898-1980) married Paul Pirtle and
they had five children: Billie Anita Pirtle Eiland, Paul Nun-
nallee, Jamie Lynn, Joe Milton and Robert Glenn. They
are buried in Salado Cemetery.

Submitted by Alice Swan Marshall

E.R. Nunnelee

Edward Richard Nunnelee was born April 19, 1882,
and reared in Austin. In 1904 he became a registered phar-
macist and located in Holland in 1905. He purchased the
Mitchell & Mewhinney store and operated this business until
1920 when he sold out to Sam Barton. In connection with
the drug business he operated the first filling station in Hol-
land about 1911. He purchased gasoline in 50-gallon bar-
rels and dispensed it from the rear of his drug store.

In 1925 Nunnelee organized the Nunnelee Drug Com-
pany and operated it until he retired in 1942. In retirement
he devoted his time to ranching. After coming to Holland,
he engaged in farming and ranching activities. He owned
one of the finest herds of registered Hereford cattle in this
section and was vitally concerned with the development of
this area.

Aimee Ida Thies and E.R. Nunnellee were married on
April 15, 1908. She was born February 7, 1881, and became
a Holland resident in 1902. She was the daughter of * Walter
G. Thies (Mar. 28, 1853-May 31, 1933). Her mother,
*Susan V. Thies, was born March 12, 1859, and died August
7, 1930.

In Holland *Mrs. Nunnelee was a member of the Meth-
odist church, Woman’s Missionary Society, Eastern Star,
and the Woman’s Study Club. She died April 22, 1951 and
*Nunnelee died January 20, 1957.

*Marked graves, Holland Cemetery

J.B. Nunneley

Jackson Baldwin Nunneley and Florence Webster mar-

Uke,

ried on September 3, 1870, in Middletown, Ohio. She was
born there on June 29, 1852, the daughter of William and
Jane Webster.

On December 25, 1871, the Nunneleys arrived in Texas
and settled at Harrisville in Bell County. This community
was 10 miles south of the present site of Temple. He became
a merchant there and served as postmaster from 1873 to
1880. His business partners included Rev. William Thomas,
T.L. Hollingsworth and James Fletcher.

The Nunneleys moved to Temple when the town started
in 1881. Prior to 1884 the pioneer firm of Nunneley and
Chattin. General Store had formed. Louis M. Chattin was
Nunneley’s partner. Nunneley built the first brick store build-
ing in Temple. He served on the city council for many years
and aided in organization of the waterworks and scores of
other projects, which promoted the city’s growth. He was
a charter member of the following organizations: Temple
Compress Company, Temple Improvement Company, The
Cemetery Company, The Electric Light Company, The Hotel
Company and Oil Mill Company. He was one of the orga-
nizers of First National Bank and served on the board of
directors. The firm of Nunneley and Chattin remained in
business for many years.

After her husband’s death, Mrs. Nunneley continued
his progressive policies toward the town and community
and gave a tile swimming pool to the YMCA. At the time
this was one of the largest public gifts made to the City of
Temple.

Nunneley, born in 1839, died in 1907. Mrs. Nunneley
died in 1928. Their marked graves are in Hillcrest Cem-
etery in Temple.

Hugh O’Keeffe

Hugh O’Keeffe was born December 25, 1804, in Kil-
rush, County Clare, Ireland, and was sent to the parish
school to be trained for the priesthood. His religious train-
ing was discontinued in 1822 when the family immigrated
to America. Hugh was described as a stocky, red-headed,
and witty Irishman.

Upon arriving in America the family settled at Ma-
dison, Indiana, where the father resumed his profession of
river boat pilot on the Ohio River. Later, they crossed over
the river into Kentucky and lived on Pattons Creek, Oldham
County. Here, Hugh married Mary Ann (Polly) Morgan
on August 30, 1826. Polly, the daughter of John Morgan,
was born on December 25, 1804, in Henry County, Kentucky.
The six children born to Hugh and Mary in Kentucky were:

1. *Ann Eliza married John Isbell Lamb.

2. *Daniel married Samantha Jane Messer, daughter
of William Tucker and Mathilda Jane Hunt Messer.

3. *Mary S. married Montgomery S. Veazey.

4. *John D. married Mary Frances Dunnegan.

5. Hugh Jr.

6. Presley.

In 1839, the family moved to Cass County, Illinois,
where Hugh farmed. While living in Illinois four more chil-
dren were born:

7. Alonzo.

8. V.B.

9. Albert Jackson.

10. Arthur H. married Sarah Kuykendall.

Cross (b. 1837) Wilbanks. Charles Asa was married at Mt.
Sylvan, near Tyler, Smith County, Texas, to Margaret
(Maggie) DeLay (Mar. 7, 1871—June 11, 1944) in Belton.
She was the second of 11 children born to Hiram Russell
Delay II (Dec. 3, 1837—Mar. 4, 1918) and Matilda Jones
DeLay (July 3, 1847—Feb. 14, 1901).

The Wilbanks, affectionately known by their children
as Papa C.A. and Mama C.A., came to Belton, Bell County,
in 1913 from Lindale, Smith County, so their oldest daugh-
ter, Leita, could attend Baylor College. When they arnved
in Belton, the new home they were having built near the
college was not quite finished, so they moved into a small
house near the Central Avenue bridge on Nolan Creek. While
they were there, a big flood washed away the bridge, the
Polk family and much of lower Belton. They lived in their
new home for many years before buying another house in
north Belton, where they lived their remaining years.

Children of Charles Asa and Margaret DeLay Wilbanks

were:

1. Fred (1899-1901).

2. Leita (d. 1982) married Hewlett D. Zachry and had
one child, Hue Leita. She later married James L. Childs.
Leita received her degree from Baylor and was a teacher.

3, Charles Ralph (Big Cotton) played football at Texas
Tech and Belton High School. He died in 1963.

- 4, Faye, living in Belton, married Albert Lloyd and had
two daughters: Betty and Ann. Faye also graduated from
Baylor College in Belton.

5. Delay Hiram (Apr. 22, 1907-— Mar. 30, 1962) was
born in Lindale and married Frankie Wilma Garner on July
16, 1938, in Belton. Frankie was the daughter of Frank Wilson
Garner (Jan. 30, 1878—July 22, 1941) and Daisie Heartfield
(Sept. 28, 1890—Aug. 26, 1974). Hiram attended North
Texas State University in Denton and was a merchant and
salesman. They had three children: Robert Lynn (b. May
13, 1939), William Lee (b. May 30, 1940) and James Ray
(b. May 27, 1941).

Hiram was known as “Little Cotton.” Wilma Garner
Wilbanks married Nelson Harris Hander (b. Feb. 26, 1909)
on April 26, 1969 in Temple. Hander was born at Perry and
they are residents of Belton.

Charlie Wilbanks was a shoe cobbler by trade and owned
his own business for many Years in Belton. Charles and Mar-
garet DeLay Wilbanks both died in Belton.

Delay Hiram Wilbanks

Delay Hiram Wilbanks, the son of Charles Asa and
Martha Cross Wilbanks, was born April 22, 1907, in Lin-
dale, Smith County. He married Frankie Wilma Garner,
the daughter of Frank Wilson Garner (Jan. 30, 1878—July
22, 1941) and Daisie Heartfield (Sept. 28, 1890—Aug. 26,
1974). They married July 16, 1938, in Belton.

Hiram attended North Texas University at Denton,
Texas, and was a merchant and salesman. They had three
children:

1. Robert Lynn (b. May 13, 1939) was born in Temple
and received a B.B.A. degree from North Texas University.
He is a district manager for Bentley Corporation in Hous-
ton. He married Frances Julia O'Neill (b. Dec. 31, 1938)

998

DeLay H. and Frankie Wilma Garner Wilbanks

on January 1, 1961, in Denton. Frances was born in Chi-
cago, Illinois. She earned her B.B.A. degree from North
Texas. They had two children: Robert Lynn Jr. (b. Oct. 6,
1961), who is an engineer for Ross-Hill Controls in Hous-
ton, married Denise Woyton (b. Dec. 3, 1960) on November
19, 1983; and Julie Delay (b. May 8, 1963) is a computer
operator at Seis-Pro Corporation in Houston.

2. William Lee (b. May 30, 1940) was born in Temple
and received his B.A. and M.A. degrees from Abilene Chris-
tian University. He also earned an M.A. degree from Sam
Houston State University and an M.A. and Ph.D. from the
State University of New York at Albany. He is a criminolo-
gist and professor at Florida International University in
Miami.

3. James Ray (b. May 27, 1941) was born in Belton.
He has a B.B.A. degree from North Texas State University.
He is a retired captain from the U.S. Marine Corps, and is
now an executive for Frito-Lay national headquarters at
Plano, Texas. He married Merritt Sue Griffin (b. Feb. 19,
1940) on April 9, 1971, in Dallas. Merritt was born in Gilmer
and has B.A. and M.A. degrees from East Texas State Uni-
versity at Commerce. They have an adopted daughter,
Amanda Sue (b. Jan. 28, 1975).

George E. Willcox

George E. Willcox was born in 1842 in Chambers
County, Texas, the son of Charles Willcox, a native of Rhode
Island, who as a young man went to sea and engaged in the
West India trade. Charles emigrated to Texas in May 1830
and started a mercantile business in Anahuac. Later, he
located his business in Galveston and continued there until
forced out during the Mexican War. Charles Willcox and
Phoebe C. Smith married in Liberty County, Texas. She
had come to Texas with her family in 1834. Charles and
Phoebe Willcox remained residents of Anahuac until his death
in 1875.

During the Civil War, George E. Willcox enlisted in
Company F, 26th Texas Cavalry, and served in Texas and
Louisiana. He participated in the Red River campaign and
was wounded at Mansfield, Louisiana. He was in Texas at
the time of the surrender, and after the war he had only his
horse. He sold the horse for $45, and with this capital, started
a mercantile business near his father’s home in December
1865. He ran this business for 18 years.

G.E. Willcox

In 1883 G.E. Willcox came to Temple and entered the
real estate business. He built several substantial buildings
and helped organize and later served as an officer in several
local corporations. These included Temple Water Works
Company, which he served as president; First National Bank,
which he helped found and served as vice president; Temple
Compress Company, which he built and served as president;
and Oil Mill Company, which he founded and served as a
director.

Willcox married M.G. Stephenson, a native Texan, in
1877. She was the daughter of Elisha Stephenson. George
and his wife had these children: George E. Jr.; Raymond;
Clyde B.; Cora E. Law; and Cleo, who married Samuel M.
Dold, then a Cole. Three of the children predeceased their
father.

On November 15, 1898, Willcox married a second time.
Mrs. Annie E. Penery had one daughter by her previous
marriage, Lillian Penery MacElveny. G.E. and Annie Willcox
had one son, E.E. Willcox. G.E. Willcox died in 1915, and
his marked grave is in Hillcrest Cemetery in Temple. He
was a member of the Knights of Pythias and Elks. He was
a supporter of the public school system and served as a
trustee. He was also a member of the Temple City Council.

Joseph Emery Willess

Joseph Emery Willess, the oldest son of Nathan Barns
Willess, was born in Bond County, Illinois, on March 18,
1838. Joseph’s mother is unknown, but his father married
Nancy Cruthis on May 23, 1843, and together they had
eight children.

In 1853, the Willess family moved to Grayson County,
Texas, but later moved to Parker, Collin, then Rockwall
Counties, where Nathan Barns Willess died May 19, 1879.
Both Joseph Emery and Nathan Willess served in the Con-
federate Army from Parker County, with Joseph remaining
in service for the duration of the Civil War.

Joseph Emery Willess married Alice Hannah Blodgett,
daughter of Francis S. and Harriett A. Blodgett, in 1860 in
Parker County. Alice was born in Illinois on January 24,
1842. and had come to Texas with her family in 1853 from
Hickory County, Missouri. Joseph E. and Alice Willess moved
to Bell County in 1868, and lived at Brookhaven until their

999

deaths. Their farm adjoined the community of Brookhaven,
and the Willess family donated land for the Brookhaven
School.

Children of Joseph and Alice Willess were:

1. Amanda Elizabeth, born in Parker County on July
1, 1861, died in Bell County on July 2, 1925. She never
married.

9. William James, born in Parker County on March
29, 1867, married Sarah Irene (Sallie) Garner on October
14, 1887. He died June 27, 1925, at Brookhaven.

3. Robert Embre, born in Parker County on January
4. 1868, married Martha Mihallie Bundrant on February
98, 1892, in Coryell County. On April 5, 1911, he married
Isabell Hill Parks. Robert died at Brookhaven on November
24, 1934.

4. Rebecca Olive, born in Bell County on April 25,
1970, married Elonzo W. Jordan on June 5, 1895. She died
August 7, 1958, at Winters, Texas.

5. Nancy Atwood, born in Bell County on January 6,
1872, married Will Carter.

6. Sarah Isabell, born in Bell County on January 6,
1875, married E. Frank Bigham. She died in Bell County
on September 7, 1967.

7. Nathan Barns, born in Bell County on April 7, 1882,
married Lou Doty. He died in west Texas in 1952.

Joseph Emery Willess died September 18, 1911, and
Alice H. Willess died July 25, 1920. Their graves were moved
from the Sugar Loaf Cemetery to Killeen Cemetery when
Fort Hood was established in 1942. The old family home-
stead is now part of the present Fort Hood reservation.

Submitted by Mrs. Johnny E. Willess

Robert Embre Willess

Robert Embre Willess, son of Joseph Emery and Alice
Hannah Blodgett Willess, was born in Parker County, Texas,
on January 4, 1868. His father and mother had moved to
Texas from Illinois in 1853, although they came with their
separate families at that time. Robert’s family moved to Brook-
haven in Bell County in 1868.

On February 28, 1892, Robert married Martha Mihallie
Bundrant in Coryell County. She was born in Coryell County
on April 1, 1875. “Hallie” Bundrant was the daughter of
Harden Alexander Bundrant, who was born in Wayne
County, Tennessee, on March 2, 1833, and died in Coryell
County on February 4, 1907. The Bundrant family came
to Texas in the early 1850s, living first in McLennan and
Bastrop Counties before settling in Coryell. Her mother was
Nancy Jane Hancock, born in Mississippi on June 8, 1840,
who died in Coryell County on March 7, 1890.

The children of Robert and Hallie Willess were all born
at Brookhaven. They were:

1. Vilas Arven, born December 7, 1893, died in Temple
in 1953. He never marnied.

2. William Robert, born September 2, 1896, married
Theresa Baldanado in Lovington, New Mexico, on September
1, 1953. He died in 1966 at Temple.

3. Harden Emery. born September 3, 1898, married
Nettie M. Blair, daughter of Francis M. and Viola Knox
Blair, on August 11, 1924, at Gatesville, Texas. He died
near Nolanville on July 21, 1964.

4. John Nathan (NJ.), born August 19, 1900, married
Addie Blanche Blair, daughter of Francis M. and Nora Knox

president and agent of the Red Cliff Silver Mining Com-\

\
Mr. F. J. Tytus is now in his seventy-seventh year,\
and is a remarkably well-preserved man. He is found\
almost every day in his office attending to the duties de-\
volving upon him as president of the Tytus Paper Com-\
pany, and is quite extensively known as “ the great paper\
manufacturer.” Mr. Tytus haz a fine reputation as a\
business man, and his career in Middletown stands above\
reproach. In 1848 he purchased a beautiful farm in the\
suburhs of the city, on which he erected a handsome and\
coummodious residence, where he now resides, surrounded\
hy every evidence of refinement and affluence.\
\
Daniel Brown Vail was born September 6, 1853, in\
Madison Township. He is a sign and ornamental painter.\

and William E. was born October 2, 1856. Ina second\
marriage Mr. Webster was united to Charlotte E. Hook\

\
George P. Webster, the nephew of Mr. Webster,\
served during the whole of the Mexican War. He also\
was in the War of the Rebellion, being shot at the bat-\
tle of Mul Springs. His rank was that of colonel, but\
he was in command of the brigade at that time. Mr.\
Webster is now engaged in the manufacture of paper\
hags, inventing the machinery himself, and employing\
sixty or seventy employees, mostly girls. The factory\
was built in 1873, but was destroyed by fire on the th\
of November, 1880. This was Saturday night, bul by\
a week from Tuesday it was again in motion as before,\
new. quarters-having been temporarily found.\
