\
His parents were William W. Vail and Lucinda Brown, County, Hl., October 11, 1856. His father is William\
\
both natives of this county. He was married October\
24, 1879, to Minnie R. Olden, daughter of David Olden\
and Celia Folk. She was born in Greenville, Darke\
County, February 13, 1858. To this union one child,\
Blanche E., was born December 26, 1880. In 1872 he\
went to Buffalo to learn a trade in the Lake Shore and\
Michigan Southern Railroad shops, where he stayed until\
the Spring of 1878, when he came back to Middletown,\
going into business for himself. He also makes excel-\
lent portraits,\
\
Hugh P. Vail was born in Middletown September 17,\
1843. His parents, Hugh Vail and Jane Porter, came\
here in 1800. At the age of sixteen he went to farming,\
continuing at this till be was thirty years old, when he\
went to teaming. He now constantly employs three\

William Webster was born in Liberty Township on\
the 2d of May, 1811, and is the son of William Web-\
ter and Mary Marsh, who came to this county from\
New Jersey in 1806. Mr. Webster was early in Jife en-\
gaged in the hardware business in Hamilton, in conjune-\
tion with his brother, Joseph Webster, but for the last\
forty years has been a resident of Middletown. He has\
been twice married. His first wife was Maria J. Ken-\
nedy, and he was married to her October 5, 1837, Her\
father’s name was Joel Kennedy, and her mother’s Esther\
Moorehouse. By her he had eight children. Albert\
Webster was born September 26, 1838, and died on the\
Sth of December, 1851; Mary was born April 12, 1840,\
and died February 7, 1841; Joel K. was born May 29,\
1843, and now lives in Kentucky; Laura J. Ford was\
born December 3, 1845, and lives in Texas; Charles\
was born November 30, 1848, and died June 27, 1868;\
Florence Nunnelly was born July 29, 1852, and lives in\

Allen Smith Wrenn, paper manufacturer, was born\
in Fairfax County, Virginia, March 6, 1815. His par-\
ents, who lived and died in Virginia, were Thomas\
Wrenn and Catherine Brent, He was married in 1845,\
in Middletown, to Parthenia Taylor, daughter of David\
Taylor and Joanna Enyart, who are both dead. Mrs.\
Wrenn was born in Middletown March 22, 1823, and\
they have had five children, Thomas A., Edward, Mary,\
Charles L., and Kaie B.\
\
Joseph Wickotf was born in New Jersey, on the 12th\
of Decemher, 1802, coming to this county in 1821.\
His parents were Samuel Wickoff and Vesta Irton.\
Mr. Wickoif was married on the &th of November,\
1838, to Eleanor Barklow, daughter of Tobias Barklaw\
and Elizabeth Jeems, who came to Butler in 1806,\
They have had six children—Vashti, Rebecca, Tobias,\
Henry, Sarah, and Joseph. Rebecca and Henry are de-\
ceased, The latter was in the army, but he was never\
heard of afterwards, and it is supposed he is dead. To-\
bias Wickoff was in the hundred-days’ service.\

reared a family of nine children. R. H. being
the eighth-born: the father was a gardener and
followed that occupation until his death, in
1890; the mother died in 1882. Young Foord
attended school in England until his seven-
teenth year, when he crossed the sea to Amer-
iea, to try his fortune in the New Republic;
he located at Elmwood, Illinois, and there began
to serve an apprenticeship at the carpenter’s
trade. He went from this place to Middletown,
Ohio, in 1861, and soon afterward took up arms
in defense of the country of his adoption; he en-
listed in the Twelfth Ohio Volunteer Infantry
for a term of three months, and at the expira-
tion of that time re-enlisted in the Thirty-tifth
Ohio Volunteer Infantry, Company K, Captain
Joel Deardorff, Colonel F. Van Deveer. His
regiment was the first to cross the Ohio river
into Kentucky and participated in the battle of
Mill Spring, where General Zollicoffer was
killed; thence he went to Nashville, Tennessee,
and afterward followed Brago’s army to Louis-
ville, Kentucky. He went into Ohio on re-
erniting serviee and afterward rejoined his reg-
iment ar Resaca, Georgia, and was ip all the
battles of the Georgia compaign, as Orderly
Sergeant of Company K; after the battle of
Chickamagua he was promoted to the office of
First Lieutenant, Company F, this honor being
conferred in recognition of gallantry in the field;
the Captain was slain, the Lieutenant absent,
and Mr. Foord had the command of his coim-
pany; after two days of battle he was wounded
and taken prisoner; he was paroled after nine
days, was sent to Camp Chase, and at the end of
tive months was exchanged. Before leaving
Ohio le was married to Miss Laura J. Webster
of Middletown, Ohio. He joined his command

at Chattanooga, just after the battle of Mission-

ary Ridge. His term of service expired at At-
lanta, in 1864, and he was sent to Chattanooga
where he was mustered out; he then went to
Middletown, Ohio, and was a resident of In-
diana until 1876, living at Richmond and In-
dianapolis.

When Mr. Foord came to Texas, in 1876, he lo-

cated at Austin and engaged in the manufacture
of paper bags; he removed to Gaiveston and re-
mained there two years. After the sale of
town lots at Temple he came to this place and
engaged in contracting and building; he erected
many of the dwellings and business houses, and
conducted a successful business until 1883. He
was then employed as bookkeeper for Nunneley
& Chattin, a position he still holds. He was
appointed Postmaster of Temple, March 3,
1889, by President Harrison; the office is well
conducted and the prompt attention of all
clerks gives nniversal satisfaction to the patrons.
Mr. Foord has served as a delegate tu the State
and county conventions of the Republican party,
He is commander of Starling Post, No. 21, G.
A. R., and takes an active interest in the organ-
ization; he isalso a member of the I. O. O. F.
and of the Elks. He owns a pleasant home in
Temple and a considerable amount of town
property.

W. PITTMAN, jeweler, Gatesville, Texas,
is one of the prosperous business men and
° highly respected citizens of the town.

Mr. Pittman was born in middle Tennessee,
April 1, 1853, the second in a family of four
children. His parents, Thomas and Mary Ann
(Haney) Pittman, natives of South Carolina,
went to Tennessee in their youth, and grew up
and married in that State. The father died in
Tennessee, in 1874. He wasa merchant. Dur-
ing the war he served for a time as Conscript-
ing Officer for the Confederate Government.
He was a Master Mason and a man who stood
high in the community in which he resided.
The mother was married a second time. Her
death occurred in 1878. Grandfather David
H. Pittman, a native of South Carolina, went to
Tennessee at an early day. He was a pioneer
farmer, school teacher and physician.

The subject of our sketch received his educa-
tion in Tennessee. In 1877 he came to Texas,
and for some time traveled over various per-
tions of the State. Finally settling down in

here. The most of this time he has been in
Gatesville, and many of the largest buildings
here, both business houses and residences, have
been erected by him. He also built bis own
home, which is among the attractive places of
the town. Mr. Gregg owns a ranch of 716
acres, on Cowhouse creek, 125 acres being
under cultivation.

He was married in 1872, to Miss Phetna
Newland, a native of Indiana, who came with
her parents to Texas when a girl. They have
six children: Florence, James Robert, Phetna
Gertrude, Samuel Bertie, Emma Alma and W.
B. Mrs. Gregg is a member of the Christian
Church. Fraternally, Mr. Gregg is associated
with the Knights of Honor.

W. RUSSELL has been a resident of Mc.
Lennan county, Texas, since 1854, and
? his example of industry, and earnest
and sincere endeavor to succeed in life, especially
in the occupation of farming, are well worthy
of imitation. He was born in Saline county,
Missouri, September 21, 1851, the seventh of
nine children born to James T. and Eva M.
(Smouse) Russell, who were natives of Mary-
land, in the monntainous regions of which
State the father followed the occupation of
farming. In 1847, he removed to Saline
county, Missouri, and five years later to Mount
Calm, Limestone county, Texas, where he pur-
chased a tract of land and resided on the same
for two years. At the end of this time he came
to McLennan county and settled on a farm that
is now occupied by the subject of this sketch,
containing 320 acres. In 1861, the father was
called from life, and the mother only survived
her husband till 1879.

When a lad, A. W. Russell was brought by
his parents to this section of country, and here
he has spent the greater portion of his life, and
closely identitied himself with its advancement
and growth. He very faithfully assisted his
father on the farm throughout his youth, and
now owns 130 acres of the home place, besides

500 acres of land in another tract, of which 250
acres are under cultivation, the annual ont-put
being from sixty to eighty bales of cotton. At
the age of fourteen years he started out in life
for himself as a dealer in stock, and by good
inanagement he soon had a large herd of horses
and cattle. After following this occupation for
about tifteen years, he gradually took up farm-
ing, and to this occupation has devoted his ~
attention ever since. Prior to the death of his
father he was given good educational advantages,
bnt all the education he has since received has
been obtained by contact with the world, the
business affairs of life, and by reading. He
was first married. in Mareh, 1873, to Miss
Emma L. Westmoreland, daughter of William
W. and Pernesia M. (Fuller) Westmoreland,
but February 5, 1890, he was called to mourn
her death. She left four children: Allie, born
in 1874, became the wife of Frank Shaw, and
is now deceased; James H., Colonel and
Daniel. Mr. Russell took for his second wife,
Miss Lula M. Luten, daughter of W. A. and
Elizabeth (Wagner) Luten, whose parents ,
moved from Missouri to Texas, in 1874, and,
after renting land for two years in DeWitt
county, purchased a farm in MeLennan county,
on which the mother died in 1890. The father
now resides in Hill connty. Mr. Russell has
one child by his present wife, Ray by name,
who was born October 4, 1891.

Mr. Russell has held the office of Deputy
Sheriff of the county for nine consecutive
years, and the office vf School Trustee for
fifteen years. He is a member of Liberty
Grove Lodge, No. 475, of the A. F. and A. M.,
at West, of which order he is a charter member.
He supports the principles of Democracy, and
is wide-awake and public-spirited.

Mary,\
\
606 N. 9th is our home! And yes, it is still beautiful. We have been restoring\
it for the last 3 years. We have torn off layers and layers of wallpaper and\
repaired and replastered it from ceiling to floor boards to it's original glory.\
We were able to restore the original entryway floor. We also put a pool and\
outdoor fireplace in which was designed to compliment the period of the\
home. Our next project is a new entryway system.\
\
We moved to Temple in 1993 from (ironically) Indiana. We moved to San\
Diego in 2001 and back in 2004. That's when we purchased 606 N. 9th. My\
husband, William, and our 3 sons love it here. We had always admired it for\
years. | am currently researching the history of this magnificent home so\
that | can get a much deserved Texas historic marker for it.\
\
| spent 4 hours in the library last night. With the help of a Temple historian,\
we were able to track all the owners of our house. The records are very\
difficult to research back that far. | have yet to go to the county offices for\
deed and tax records.\
\
| have a few questions....\
4. From what | can tell so far, Richard Foord looks to have built our home. Is\
that correct??? If so, what is the exact year?\
\
2. Did Richard, Laura, Jackson, and Florence all live together here at the\
same time??? The city directories indicated that.\
\
3. In 1939, a W. E. Webster resided here. Is he related to Florence Webster\
Nunneley??\
\
lam so excited about this research. | would LOVE to find old\
Foord/Nunneley family photos and display them in the house. | have\
enclosed a drawing and a recent photo of our home during our historic 4th of\
July parade. We are board members of in the Temple Historic Preservation\
League. We host many neighborhood outings throughout the year on our\
front lawn and pool area.\
\
Jane Hunt\

Jane Hunt <texasdoors@hot.rr.com> wrote:\
\
Regarding your below post about Jackson Nunneley: Was Jackson related to Laura J. Nunneley\
Foord from Temple, TX??? | am researching my 100+ home and his name was listed as living\
here...... Please help.\

Jane\

Greg,\
\
Saw your posting concerning Sheriff James David Nunneley of Milam Co. TX, born 4 Mar 1837 in\
Carroll Co. AR. | doubt that the initial D stood for David. James D. had a brother David born 1824 in\
Hickman Co. TN, died 1843 in probably Carroll or Franklin Co. AR. James D. Nunneley was the son\
of Joseph Alexander Nunneley (son of Anderson Nunneley and Martha Baldwin) and Nancy Easley\
(dau of Thomas Easley and Ecleo Wade). | have a picture taken by Jackson Baldwin Nunneley, a\
photographer, brother of James D. Nunneley and it is not identified but surely a Nunneley. Contact me\
for further info concerning this family and a possible location to search for a picture in Bell Co. TX.\

Subject: Re: Jackson B. Nunneley\

Jane,\
‘The stone at Hillcrest Cemetery in Temple, Texas is engraved as follows:\
\
LAURA JANE / WIFE OF CAPT.R. H. FOORD / 1845 - 1917\
\
The online posting for Hillcrest Cemetery shows her name to be Laura Jane Nunneley, wife of Cap. R. H.\
Foord. This is an error. Laura Jane was the daughter of William and Mariah Webster. She married Richard\
H. Foord/Ford on 24 Dec 1863 in Butler County Ohio. Laura and Richard moved to Indiana by 1870 and finally\
to Temple, Bell Co. TX. She was the sister of Florence Webster Nunneley, wife of Jackson Baldwin Nunneley.\
| visited Hillcrest Cemetery in Temple several years ago (it is beautiful) and took pictures of the tombstones of\
the Foords and Nunneleys. The posting on the internet for Hillcrest is a great site for historians and\
genealogists. As a member of the White Rose Cemetery Board in Bartlesville, Oklahoma, | must tell you that\
"cleaning up" the records for an old cemetery is a huge job and one in which | am now involved with our\
Board. The following are the listings for the Foord and Nunneley stones:\
\
Jackson Baldwin Nunneley / 1839-1907\
THIS IS CORRECT\
\
Florence / Wife of J. B. Nunneley / 1852-1928\
THIS IS CORRECT\
\
Unknown Nunneley / no dates / D.A.R.\
\
THIS IS INCORRECT...IN FRONT OF THE LARGE NUNNELEY MARKER IS A MARKER WITH THE DAR\
SYMBOL AND SAYS "BETTY MARTIN CHAPTER DAR" THIS IS A MEMORIAL FOR THE MEMBERSHIP\
OF FLORENCE WEBSTER NUNNELEY IN DAR\
\
Laura Jane Nunneley / 1845-1917 / wife of Cap. R. H. Foord\
THIS IS INCORRECT. THE STONE ACTUALLY SAYS, LAURA JANE / WIFE OF CAPT. R. H. FOORD /\
1845-1917. SHE WAS NOT A NUNNELEY, BUT A WEBSTER\
\
Capt. R. H. Foord / 1841-1916\
THIS IS CORRECT\
\
Jackson Nunneley was a prominent citizen of Temple. From various sources | found while visiting Temple, he\
was involved in the following:\
\
Owned, with L. M. Chattin, a mercantile business (they built the first brick building in Temple)\
\
Owned paper bag companies in Austin and Galveston\
\
Charter member of |.0.0.F /K of P/ B.P.O.E\
\
Helped organize the Temple Water Works Co.\
\
Director of the Temple First National Bank until his death\
\
Charter member of the Temple Compress Company / Temple Improvement Company / the Electric Light\
Company the Hotel Company / and the Oil Mill Company.\
\
Charter member of the City Cemetery Company (now Hillcrest)\
\
Hope this helps correct your information. If you ever visit Temple, do go to the cemetery and also, you can see\
the lovely home of Jackson and Florence at 606 North 9th. It is well maintained, or was a few years ago.\
\
Mary R M Nunneley\
Bartlesville Oklahoma\

\
fellowship hall were dedicated on March 15, 1959. Rev. Ba-\
letka also started the plans for the east wing, which was\
dedicated on April 16, 1967, under the pastorate of Rev.\
Eugene Kotrla. From 1969-1979, Rev. Albert Michalik\
served the church. For a few months Chaplain James Parks\
served the church until the present pastor, Rev. Mark Labaj\
came to serve full-time. The church now has 236 members.\
\
The church gives support to community agencies such\
as the Temple Family Services, TreeHouse, Half-way House,\
Harvest House, Veterans Administration volunteer services,\
U.S.O., the nursing homes, and other projects through the\
local organization of the church women of Temple.\
\
Christ Episcopal Church\
\
In 1883, about 15 Episcopalians in the two-year-old\
town of Temple began meeting for worship in schools and\
vacant store buildings. They were assisted by itinerate mis-\
sionary priests such as Rev. Mr. Wey and Rev. Mr. Hogue.\
\
In 1887, in cooperation with the Rt. Rev. Alexander\
Gregg, bishop of the Diocese of Texas, they secured a lot\
on present Fifth Street near Avenue A and in 1890, erected\
a small, simple frame building. It was designed by a devout\
Episcopalian, Mrs. Seaborn Green, who also suggested a\
name for the mission: Christ Episcopal Church. Rev. J.A.\
Duncan became first missionary priest for Temple in 1889,\
also serving other area missions and churches. In 1892, the\
mission was admitted to council in the Diocese of Texas.\
\
In a town with a large itinerant population of railway\
workers, the church grew slowly,but several major develop-\
ments occurred during the ministry of the Rev. Thomas J.\
Sloan, 1899-1906. The church building and rectory was\
moved to Calhoun Avenue between Main and First Streets\
in 1901. In 1902, Christ Church was admitted to council\
as a parish, and in 1904-1905 the present church buildling\
was erected at the corner of North Main Street and Cal-\
houn Avenue. It was constructed of Bell County hard lime-\
stone in a Gothic Revival style resembling a typical English\
parish church. In 1913, the debt on the building was paid,\
and it was dedicated and consecrated by the Rt. Rev. George\
Herbert Kinsolving, Bishop of the Diocese of Texas.\
\
The church barely held its own until 1930 when the\
Rev. Joseph B. Dobbins became rector. The church grew\
in numbers, activities and spiritual attainments, and was\
financially sound. Several innovations were introduced, such\
as publishing a weekly parish bulletin and conducting Christ-\
mas Eve midnight services. World War II presented new\
challenges and opportunities, with Camp Hood nearby and\
an army hospital in Temple. Christ Church had a special\
opportunity to serve a great influx of Episcopalians. In 1942,\
a parish house was constructed. It was enlarged and class-\
rooms were added in 1956. In 1957, a parochial school, long\
desired by Rev. Dobbins, was established. It was named\
McGowen-Stephens School] in 1973. Rev. Dobbins retired
in 1966, after 35 years of ministry to the church.

In 1966, several families from Christ Church founded
St. Francis Mission in southwest Temple. A new rectory
was built in 1967, replacing one purchased in 1953. In 1969
and 1970, major restoration of the church building saved
it from rapid deterioration. These projects were accomplished
during the rectorship of Rev. Donald C. Blavier. In 1970,
Rev. Joseph DiPaola became rector, and in 1973, the church
purchased the remainder of the city block on which it is
located. In 1976, a historical marker and medallion were

199

awarded to the church. At the beginning of 1985, member-
ship in the church number 671.
Submitted by Weldon G. Cannon

Chupik Corporation
S.F. Chupik Cabinet Shop was founded in 1929 and

has grown to become a major distributor and manufacturer
of custom architectural] millwork.

Steve F. Chupik opened the small blacksmith and cab-
inet shop in a metal building with a dirt floor just off East
Adams Avenue in Temple. He had come to Texas in 1887
at the age of four from Ratibar, Moravia. In 1935, he sold
half interest to his second oldest son, Henry Emil Chupik
and the business was renamed S.F. Chupik & Son Cabinet
Shop. The Chupiks purchased the Viaduct Lumber & Plan-
ing Mill in 1940, and during the war they manufactured
ammunition boxes and foot lockers.

In 1946, the business was divided and expanded to in-
clude distribution with branch warehouses in Fort Worth
and San Antonio. That year the name was again changed,
this tme to Chupik Wood Manufacturing Company, Inc.
In the early 1950s, S.F. Chupik retired. In the mid-1960s,
Henry’s oldest son, H. Wayne Chupik joined the firm and
the plant was relocated to a 10-acre site on IH-35 in 1967.
By then the company had distribution centers in Houston,
Temple, Lubbock, Dallas and Austin.

In the mid-1970s, Henry Emil Chupik retired, and he
now serves as chairman of the board of the corporation.
H. Wayne Chupik is president of Chupik Corporation, the
name it has had since 1964.

Church Of The Living God

The local Church of the Living God was organized in
1907 by Sister White and Elder Smith, the pastor. There
were 12 charter members, all of whom are now deceased.

The husband of one charter member, Nealie Steward
is still living; Herman Steward is the oldest member and
still attends regularly.

Eldora Blackmond has been honored with the designa-
tion of ‘‘Mother”’ of the Temple church.

At first the church held services under a tent, but later
a building was erected on the same spot. The church 1s now
affiliated with the General Assembly of the Church of the
Living God which has member churches throughout the
United States.

The presiding bishop is C.C. Berry, Jr., with headquar-
ters in Dallas; district bishop is D.C. Moore of Houston,
and Elder Nathaniel Harris is pastor of the Temple church.

Submitted by Sister A. Benson

City Federation Of Women’s Clubs

There have been two City Federation of Women’s Clubs
in Temple. The first was founded on March 29, 1900, by
the Self Culture Club and the Women’s Literary Club. The
primary goal of this organization was to secure a grant from
the Carnegie Foundation to establish a public library for
Temple.

Onginal officers were: Mrs. W.S. Banks, president; Mrs.
Edward Wynne, first vice president; Mrs. J.B. Blair, second
vice president; Miss Lucy MacGregor, recording secretary;
Mrs. J.B. Nunneley, treasurer; Miss Jo Cater and Miss Carne

Scott, librarians; Miss Lucy MacGregor and Miss Blanche
Haller, volunteer assistant librarians.

The Federation's other major project was establishment
of a Women’s Rest Room for rural women who came to
town with their husbands. The retreat provided baby beds,
couches, and other things to give the women a place to relax.
It soon became known throughout the state.

After $15,000 was received from the Carnegie Founda-
tion for the library project, the Federation was absorbed
into the Carnegie Public Library Association, which had
Mrs. Banks as president and Miss MacGregor as secretary.

On November 30, 1915, Mrs. C.L. Myers and Mrs.
Charles S. Cox reorganized the City Federation of Women’s
Clubs at a meeting in the library. Organizations which sent
representatives to the meeting included: Daughters of the
American Revolution, Carnegie Library Association; Civic
League, Delta Zeta, Domestic Science Club, Housekeepers
Club, Woman’s Study Club, and Woman’s Suffrage Club.

One project of the group, the Everywoman’s Party, lasted
46 years, from 1916-1962.

During the presidency of Mary Louise de Grummond,
the idea of a club house was started. In 1946-47, a lot was
purchased, and the next year, the club began construction.
The keys to the club house were presented to Mrs. J.E.
Johnson, club president, in the fall of 1948.

In addition to its civic endeavors which are numerous,
the club has two annual projects. The Louzelle Rose Barclay
Contests award prizes for accomplishments in the fields of
art, literature and music. The Temple Authors’ Collection
is the second project. The club also provides two Temple
Junior College scholarships.

Submitted by Mrs. Bert E. (Lois) Booth

Charles S. Cox Men’s Clothing

The Charles S. Cox Men’s Clothing opened April 1903,
on Avenue A behind the First National Bank and then moved
to 8 South Main in Temple. The son was given a third inter-
est in 1925, and the name was changed to Charles S. Cox
& Son. The firm was sold in 1952.

Charles S. Cox, Sr. and Ann Burge worked for Bentley
Bass and Co. prior to their marriage. As partners, they opened
the men’s clothing store and ran it for 49 years.

Submitted by Patsy Cox Hinkle

Daughters of the American Revolution
Betty Martin Chapter

On April 6, 1904, Mrs. Huling B. Robertson organized
the Betty Martin Chapter of the Texas Society of the Daugh-
ters of the American Revolution with 17 members, the elev-
enth Texas chapter. The chapter was named for a pre-
revolutionary relative of Mrs. Robertson’s.

Charter members were: Mrs. Huling B. (Mary G.C.)
Robertson, Mrs. Will W. (Mary Ferman) Hair, Miss Kate
Butler, Mrs. F.F. (Elma Keys) Downs, Mrs. F.E. (Annie
Butler) Merrill, Mrs. M. (Aria Pendleton) Lyster, Mrs. N.A.
(Mable Pendleton) Sayre, Mrs. W.C. (Sue Attie) Camp-
bell, Mrs. C.V. (Carrie V.) Cheatman, Mrs. W. Goodrich
(Zollie Luther) Jones, Mrs. Hugh A. (Rose Stallings) Leake,
Mrs. C.W. (Mary McFarland) Peyton, Mrs. Lee F. (Myrtle
Pendleton) Cowan, Mrs. J.C. (Scott Talley) Mitchell, Mrs.
G.W. (Effie Butler) Nettleton, and Mrs. J.M. (Anna Bur-
bank) Woodson.

200

The Priscilla Alden Society, Children of the American
Revolution was founded and sponsored by the Betty Marun
Chapter in 1910. In 1959, Mrs. Richard D. Haines was the
author and recipient of the first Valley Forge Freedom Founda-
tion award given by any society of the C.A.R. She presented
this award to the NSDAR museum for display in honor of
the Texas Society of the Daughters of the American Revo-
lution.

In 1917, a flag pole and marker were placed on Temple
municipal grounds in memory of Woodrow Wilson. It was
rededicated in 1930. This chapter also placed a marker at
Salado at the site of the old Salado College which was estab-
lished in 1860.

During World Wars I and II, the Betty Martin Chap-
ter sponsored many services to the war effort, selling bonds,
raising money, knitting sweaters, working through the Red
Cross, planting victory gardens, and volunteering services
to McCloskey General Hospital patients. The chapter con-
tinues to be of service to veteran patients.

The U.S. Bicentennial project in 1976, was helping the
new Railroad and Pioneer Museum to sort, accession and
catalog the museum’s holdings. Also as a Bicentennial pro-
ject, a history of the chapter was compiled by Mrs. F.H.
Daniel.

In 1978, Earnest Fletcher established a fund in perpe-
tuity in honor of his mother, Belle McDonald Fletcher, a
former regent. The fund provides for a scholarship to be
awarded each year to an outstanding American history stu-
dent.

The chapter observes Arbor Day each year by ; .anting
trees throughout the city. This chapter continues to strive
to promote the objectives of the National Society; historic
preservation, education, and patriotism. The chapter has
142 members.

Daughters of the Republic of Texas
Ben Milam Chapter

Ben Milam Chapter, Daughters of the Republic of Texas,
was founded on April 21, 1899, the eighth chapter to be
organized. The organization followed a meeting of the Texas
Veterans Association in Temple at the old Central Hotel.

Mrs. Annie Moore Minnis was the first president; Mrs.
Annie Ayers Booker, vice president; Mrs. Myrtle Allen, sec-
retary; and Mrs. R.D. Field, treasurer. There were 15 mem-
bers. When Mrs. Minnis and Mrs. Field moved away after
a few years, and four other members died, the chapter dis-
banded.

In the fall of 1921, an invitation from Mrs. Eli Town-
send of Baylor College in Belton was sent out to the women
in Bell County who were eligible for membership in the DRT.
Members of the Ben Milam Chapter in Temple accepted
the invitation. After some discussion, the name of Ben Milam
was chosen, with meetings to alternate between Temple and
Belton.

On May 31, 1933, the Belton women voted to organize
their own chapter, choosing the name of Sam Houston. Mrs.
Louis H. Tyler was elected president.

The Sam Houston Chapter and the Ben Milam Chap-
ter continued to share in major projects. One such project
was the erection of a marker on the banks of the Leon River
which marked the site of the first election held in Bell County
in 1850. This was one of the projects to mark the Texas

Harrisville

Harrisville, an early day stage stop, was located about
10 miles south of the present site of Temple. There was a
store, saloon, gin, blacksmith shop, and photographic shop
there.

On February 22, 1869, James B. Nunneley was ap-
pointed postmaster for the village. Other postmasters and
their appointment dates were: Charles A. Everett, July 7,
1870; George C. Harmon, October 17, 1871; Jackson B.
Nunneley, May 4, 1874; J.B. Nunneley, August 10, 1881;
Thomas L. Hollingsworth, August 31, 1881; and James E.
Thomas, February 15, 1882. Rogers Post Office began ser-
vice to the Harrisville residents on October 24, 1882.

J.B. Nunneley, Rev. William Thomas, and T.L. Hol-
lingsworth were business partners in Harrisville. Nunneley
moved to Temple when that town was started in 1881, and
Hollingsworth also later located in Temple.

Howard

Howard, or Old Howard as it was sometimes known,
grew up along one of the roads which connected the county
seat with all the small communities. Howard was on the
“Waco Village to Georgetown’ road which followed the
old military road from Howard via Belton and Salado.

Until 1854, only three communities in Bell County had
post offices: Belton, Howard and Salado. On April 29, 1852,
Moses M. Turney was appointed postmaster at Howard.
Those who followed included: James A. Graves, December
9, 1852; Silas Baggett, December 18, 1855 and January 17,
1862; R« .ert M. Moore, March 16, 1866; Edmund G. Pend-
leton, Fe »cuary 3, 1874; Samuel J. Brown, January 24, 1876;
George UC. Pendleton, January 2, 1879; S.J. Brown, March
17, 1879; Daniel R. Pendleton, January 10, 1883; David R.
Pendleton, February 2, 1883; James Joyce, March 27, 1886;
and Thomas L. Grace, March 1, 1887. The Howard post
office was discontinued in 1883 and mail was delivered by
the Belton post office. In 1884, the Howard post office was
re-established, but finally discontinued when the Pendle-
tonville post office began to serve the area in 1889.

In the days of the stagecoach and cattle drives, Howard
served as a stage stop and was one of the focal points where
drovers collected their herds for the month-long routes to
the Kansas markets.

In those days, the old Silas Baggett home stood like a
sentinel atop a hill overlooking miles of prairie country. Sam
Brown and Henry was one of the stores, and there was a
saloon.

The cattle were corraled in the inspection pens await-
ing their drives northward. Tom Elliott, who lived at Old
Howard, recalled having heard of $10,000 changing hands
in one day in some of the cattle deals.

There was no hotel in Howard, but the Silas Baggett
home was open to travelers. George Pendleton, who became
lieutenant governor of Texas, once lived in the Howard com-
munity.

Time has wiped out most of the landmarks of the com-
munity, leaving no sign of the early village on the hill.

Keys Vailey

The land in this secluded valley, named for an early
pioneer family, was the first to be inundated for the Stillhouse
Hollow Reservoir. The early settlers in Keys Valley were:

25%

)

aod

V.H. Anderson, Fred C. Brookman, William Brookman,
W.H. Bruce, William Ferguson, Wesley G. Ferguson, Sam
C. Hannon, Hal Keys, Thomas Jefferson McKee, W.L.
Pierce, Newton M. Proctor, Sr., Gordon W. Shanklin, and
Howell Tatum.

Soon after their arrival, a combination school and church
house was erected where children from this valley and from
the eastern part of Wilkinson Valley, just across the Lam-
pasas River, attended the one-room school. In the summer
time, church services were held under a brush arbor. After
the school was consolidated with another district, the Bap-
tists built a house of worship.

As time passed, other families increased the population
of Keys Valley. They were named Baggett, Copeland,
Damron, Hamilton, McCrackin, Poe, Ross, Safley and
Sparks.

Four roads were built by workmen with mule teams
from the county. The residents of the valley assisted by pro-
viding labor, teams, wagons, picks and shovels for the upkeep
of the roads.

The Stillhouse Hollow road, cut from the east side of
the hollow, was steep and narrow. It was considered the
main road, and farmers going to market with wagon loads
of cotton and corn had to rest their teams on the long pull.

The Keys Valley Cemetery was moved to Cedar Knob
Cemetery when the government acquired the land for the
reservoir.

The Keys Valley Baptist Church was also relocated
to Belton.

Little Flock
[This is an excerpt from a speech written by Grace Horstman and\
read by Gaylon Christie at the May 1969 dedication of Little Flock’s\
Texas Historical Marker. ]

“I’m sure when James W. Moore and his wife, Mary
Anne Henson Moore, with her parents, Mr. and Mrs.
Henson, sailed from England about 1840, they could not
imagine how their future lives would develop. Their boat
docked on the coastline of Virginia and North Carolina.
After living in that area four years, they moved on south
to Georgia for one year, then to Alabama for one year, and
on to Batson’s Prairie near Meridian, Mississippi. They lived
at Batson three years. After seven weeks of travel by wagon
train and crossing the Mississippi River on a ferry boat,
they settled at Bastrop, Texas. During the one year of farm-
ing and ranching there, both parents of Mary Anne died
and were buried there.”

The Moores’ next home for one year was in the Sun-
shine Community northeast of present Holland. This land
had been vacant for the last 10 years, because of the severe
Indian attacks and massacres. The Moores’ last move was
about 1855, to the Little Elm Creek area, known now as
Little Flock.

All these moves were made by wagons with oxen, mule
and horse teams. The slaves led or drove the stock behind
the wagon train. One favorite slave felt honored that his
duty was to lead a black jack all the trip.

“Uncle Jimmy” and “‘Aunt Molly,” as they were fondly
called, even by their grandchildren, bought 1000 acres of
ranch land for 50 cents per acre. The original house was
built on Elm Creek of split logs in two sections joined by a
roofed hall with no doors at either end, commonly spoken
of as a ‘dog trot.”’ In their home there was evidence of their

' The death of Dr. A.D. Woods on Janaury 26, 1901,
left the Donahoe community in Bell County without a doctor.
In January 1902, Dr. and Mrs. Flaniken came to Denahoe
to look for a place to live. In March their home was com-
pleted and they moved from Oenaville. In later years the
Flanikens moved to Rogers.

Dr. Flaniken rode a horse to visit his patients, his instru-
ments hung on one side of the horse in a sheepskin chamois
and a case of medicine hung on the other side. Many times
he had to swim his horse across Donahoe Creek and Little
River when they were out of their banks. He was paid with
butter, corn, chickens, calves and others items.

Texas Reginald Flaniken died in 1942 and Anna Fla-
niken died in 1959. Their marked graves are in Val Verde
Cemetery.

Submitted by Tex R. Flaniken

James and Mary E. Marshall Fletcher
James Fletcher

James Fletcher, a Bell County farmer, was born in Ar-
kansas Territory on April 2, 1822, the son of Robert and
Mary Wilson Fletcher. Robert Fletcher (1774-1856) was
born in Augusta or Rockbridge County, Virginia, and mar-
ried Mary Polly Wilson (1795— Nov. 30, 1878) about 1810
in Kentucky.

James Fletcher was a colonist in the Republic of Texas.
According to Land Certificate No. 573, County of Harris-
burg, James appeared before the Board of Land Commis-
sioners and stated he arrived in the Republic subsequent
to the declaration of independence, and that he was a single
man. The grant of 640 acres was to be surveyed after August
1, 1838; however, it was never completed because he re-
turned to his father’s home in Arkansas.

Fletcher returned to Texas in 1844 with members of a
Marshall family. They came prospecting and during this
time stayed with the John Leggett Marshall family in Fay-
ette County. Later, they came to what is now Bell County
where they were subjected to attacks from hostile Indians
and had frequent encounters with Mexicans, who would
steal their livestock.

After being here for about a year, the men returned to
Arkansas to bring their families to Texas and they acquired
more livestock, which they drove back with them. They set-
tled in Bell County and purchased land; however, the ttles
proved faulty and they were forced to buy land again in a
few years.

During the Civil War, Fletcher served about five months
with the militia and was detailed to supply cattle to the
army. After returning home, he resumed farming activities.
In the early 1870s he built a store south of present-day Hei-
denheimer and operated it for several years. The store was
located on a stagecoach stop between Dallas and Houston
and was called Old Harrisville. His partner in the opera-
tion was J.B. Nunnally, who later moved to Temple when
that new town was founded.

When Little River was founded, Fletcher moved there
and built the first store. He operated this business unt the
building burned in 1884, after which he returned to his agri-
cultural pursuits.

James Fletcher and Mary Elizabeth Marshall marred
on May 25, 1851, in Bell County. She was born January
92, 1825, in Illinois, the daughter of Robert Marshall (b.
Dec. 3, 1775) of South Carolina. The Marshalls are buried
in marked graves at Old Fort Griffin Cemetery.

James Fletcher died July 11, 1903, and his wife died
June 2, 1897. Their marked graves are in Hillcrest Cem-
etery in Temple. They had three children:

1. Robert Pleasant (Feb. 3, 1855—July 10, 1858).

2. James Andrew

3, Mary E. (b. Mar. 16, 1860) married Eli Dougherty
on December 28, 1876. She died July 9, 1911. Dougherty
was born January 28, 1850 and died November 1927. He
was a merchant in Temple and owned a farm. They were
parents of: Mrs. E.J. Burns, J.R. Dougherty, Mrs. B.H. Wil-
liams and Mrs. J.P. McKeehan. The Doughertys are buried
in Hillcrest Cemetery in Temple.

Submitted by Mrs. Joe N. Weatherby

James Andrew Fletcher

James Andrew Fletcher was born January 31, 1858,
at his parents’ farm near the present location of Academy
School in Bell County. He was the son of James and Mary
Elizabeth Marshall Fletcher. He attended Little River com-
munity schools and for a short time attended Trinity Uni-
versity at Waxahachie, Texas.

On October 31, 1878, Fletcher married Susan Jane
Greathouse at her family’s plantation south of Temple. She
was born May 8, 1861, in Notasulga, Alabama, daughter

Aap of the immediate Central Texas showing Temple, Texas and most of
.er neighboring towns, villages and communities.

IGF CxO

In the process of gathering and storing of hard facts in the Prairie
Queen’s historical granary, some discrepancies may be found. At times
even members of pioneer families may differ in certain facts, certain
dates, or the unraveling of some keen story. But this isn’t all bad. For the
main purpose of this flaring treatise is to get you and you to cipher and
decipher, spell and dispel, cuss and discuss the conflicts of certain
transactions as well as the wonderous achievements of our history-
making colonists of yesteryears.

So as we look back to the servants of the Prairie Queen of yesteryears,
yesterdays, and even today, let’s ‘‘doff our hats’’ as we stroll down her
Memory halls.

XII

CHAPTER ONE

- Whenever the Gulf, Colorado, and Santa Fe Railroad and the Missouri,
Kansas and Texas Railway crossed tracks in Temple, those steel sparks
set off a magical spell of commerce and industry, plus all the trimmings
that has made her the leading city of our country and the immediate
Central Texas. It was the ‘‘New York’’ of Bell County. However, the
almost combined cities of Killeen and Harker Heights may question this
and rightfully so, since they are growing so rapidly, due partially to the
coming of Fort Hood.

From the very beginning Old Nesters laughed about Temple looking so
boyish, green, beardless and unlicked behind the ears. Why the very idea
was enough to make you “‘slap your grandma down."’ So called smart
railroad men... men‘of books schooled in mathematics, science, and
topography attempted to layout a city on a bald prairie with no more
water than a weak spring or two and a little boggy creek lined with green
willows and croaking frogs.

Such tomfoolery when there was Belton some six miles west, a proven
town with untold barrels of spring water that had been discovered by
Philip Nolan for whom Nolan Creek was named.

Look again at the facts, the town of Belton had been proven. It had
withstood the rigors of the Civil War with its buildings of lime-chinked
rock and cedar-studded.

For factual claim to such remarks, consider the Texas Almanac of 1859,
by Forsgard G. A. And James Burke... . Book-sellers and Station-
ers... . Houston, Texas. Also, Agents for I. M. Singer and Co's
Celebrated Pianos, Bell County:

In 1859, 4 grist and 2 saw mills, run by horse power, corn made 30 bu.
to acre, land improved 5 to 10 dollars an acre, Chinese sugar cane 150
gals. per acre.

- 3,828 horses valued at $164,006.
- 778 Negroes, $434,125.00
- Land 225,550 acres, valued at $585,268.00.
- Beef worth 2%¢ per lb.
- 1700 sheep, $3.50 per head
- Wool, 25¢ per lb.
Belton, only town in Bell County, population 300 people.
- Stone court house, 50 ft. x 60 ft., 4 rooms below and a stairway to
court room.
- Oil spring in county, good for healing old sores, looks tar in
appearance.
- Ys of county ts hilly.
- Pine for buildings sold for $4.50 per 1,000’s.
County Officers:

A.K. Ramsey - Chief Justice, John C. Caddel - Clerk of County Court

Joe Cater - Sheriff, M.W. Dameron - Assessor and Collector, A.W

Coret the MK-T built a pretty white depot, baggage facilities and all.
It was very modern for the time.

“Somewhat later Pete Riola built a sturdy red brick grocery store across
the tracks since the compress had burned. Salvaging some of the
compress bricks, the store had walls 15 inches thick. Such thickness was
obtained with a three bricker layer and plenty of cement plaster. The total
cost of the store was $1,800. The Riola family lived in the back and ran
the store in front. Today that Riola store still stands, weary but sturdy.

“Mr. Pete Riola was a first cousin to Frank Floca. Frank Floca ran a
grocery store on Wall Street and Avenue B.

Mr. Sam Cortese ran a grocery store between Fourteenth and
Sixteenth Street on Avenue C. Mr. Cortese was the father of Mrs. Frank
Floca.

The Prairie Queen had scarcely staked and erected her temporary tent
alongside of Knobb creek in 1881, whence came along two ambitious and
farsighted gents. Among other worthwhile things, they decided to built
themselves a private bank. It was first called the Bell County Bank.
However, it was better known as Downs Brothers’ Bank. The two young
men at the time were P.L. (Pink) Downs and F.F. (Fort) Downs. They
were fine-hewed, men of a rare sort, quite conspicuous as they dealt in
various town enterprises. Too, they were ‘some dressers’’, always spick
and span, neat -- neat as a new boot. Later on they buggyed about town
wearing white shirts with E and W collars and cuffs, flowing ties and all
of this was covered over with all wook serge suits.

But in the beginning of their first town venture, some of this had not
come along -- for in 1882, they were two romping young men, no doubt in
blue denim shirts, duck pants and tight galluses which was footed by
cowhide boots or brogans. Having built their stout, clap-boarded bank,
which nowadays is called a shack, but in 1882 the structure was counted
quite classic. With everything in order, they left their 20 ft. by 30 ft.
structure and headed for Waco village in a wagon to fetch in a load of cold
cash. On the way back, their wagon bogged in the muddy trail of a road
between Eddy and Troy. Having a few quilts along and several messes of
tack they spent the night in the wagon for they dared not to leave the
money. The next morning they hired a boost-team from a prairie farmer.
Having reached higher ground, they rolled into Temple and placed the
money in their bank, which is now two doors north of the present First
National Bank. In fact, it is now (1975) the J.C. Penny Co.

So Temple’s first bank was in being and ready for business. The bank
had a cash capital of $10,000. This same year, 1882, Temple had a big
barbecue to celebrate its birthday. On this day there were $100,000 worth
of town lots sold. The average price was $50. In 1884, this Bell County
_ bank was nationalized with a capital stock of $50,000. And for this early

| day in time, of 1884, this was indeed a heaping pile of money. Some of
' the most powerful welding men in the Prairie Queen's township became

46

associated with it. Besides F.F. Downs as president (he was the eldest
brother) P.L. Downs served as cashier and later as vice-president and
still later he was on the board of directors. However, under the original
set-up, George Wilcox was vice-president and having F.E. Sanford
cashier. Other directors were J.B. Nunnelley, Otto K. Burwitz and E.B.
Baggett. .

The Downs brothers were talented hands in making Temple a
powerhouse even from a mud hole setting to a growing city. Elsewhere
has been given about some of their business affairs, their social life and
their loyalty to their lodges. F.F. Downs home at 212 North 9th Street was
one of Temple’s earliest fine homes. Material, the finest, came by wagon
train from Galveston. His residence at one time was also 14 South 6th
Street. P.L. Downs, Sr.’s residence was 600 North lst Street. P.L.
Downs, Jr. lived on South 43rd Street.

From such an humble, yet positive beginnings, the Downs Brothers’
Bank became the First National.

And now a few more accountings of the fine family of Mr. and Mrs.
Frank Floca. That’s right. Their grocery store was at 2nd Street and Wall
Street (Avenue B). They were good business people, quite thrifty, too.
Mr. Floca, coming to Temple when the Prairie Queen had hardly seated
herself, knew that she was bound to launch out, going from gingham and
calico to satin and silk. And Mr. Floca always kept a lookout eye for
worthy undertakings. So one day he upheaped and bought the downtown
Bell Theater building at about 16 South Main Street. Several enterprises
romped at this location at different times. On one side of it was a barber
shop and on the other was the popular Wildcat Newstand at one time.

In the meantime, or more likely before this deal, Mr. Floca took on the
whole brick building along Avenue B from the alley to 2nd Street. Some
of them were two story buildings. Those buildings served well various
business concerns such as drugstores, dental offices, Berry’s Feed Store,
Hawkin’s Grocery and so forth.

The Floca boys had a great teacher in their daddy. However, he
continued to buy real estate. He bought the vacant lot in front of his
business houses on the corner of Avenue B and 2nd Street. Here was
built the big Western State Wholesale house. Later it was torn down and
still later on came the Temple National Bank’s drive-in banking facilities,
the first one in town.

The sidewalks alongside the Floca buildings were laid in 1907 and '08
by E. Blankstein. The building on the corner at 201 South Main Street
was probably not owned by Mr. Floca. Mr. J.N.Jepson built this strong
brick house. His name is embedded in the iron mat at the entrance. The
sidewalks here also were built in 1907. On this corner, there used to be
plenty of iron rings to tie horses. In 1910, the sidewalks were extended 4
and 5 feet into the streets thereby making wide pedestrian walkways and
narrow streets.

CHAPTER TI\
\
In the early going of the Prairie Queen, no fine home was complete\
unless it was surrounded by a white picket fence. Others had netted wire\
and iron barriers. Here are some of those beautiful homes and a\
descriptive word or two will be given, not only about the dwelling but also\
their owners. These residential homes were built from 1890 to about\
1920. After 1920 beautiful brick homes began to appear.\
\
The following homes were built from 1890 to 1910. Many of them were.\
\
two story, having 16 and 18 feet ceilings. Some of these homes have been\
mentioned before. They are mentioned again as a sequence and also for\
emphasis.\
\
A.C. Buchanan in 1895 lived in a spacious two story house. It was a\
pretty thing with a graduating ledged roof. Mr. Buchanan wore a\
mustache; his hair was thinning; he always looked fit; and he was so\
active in the early years of Temple. .\
\
Dr. E.T. Walker lived in a big two story home. In his residence was the\
beginning of King’s Daughters Hospital. Dr. Walker was fairly tall, quite\
slender, and wore a big mustache. .\
\
City Marshall, Wm. Taylor, was a husky man. He resided at 21 South\
4th Street, now the Campbell Bruce Lumber Company.\
\
The Kyle Boarding House, 10th and Avenue A. was a fancy place.\
George Kyle was dapper-looking, so youngish and active. Remember this\
was in 1895. ; .\
\
W.H. Crane’s home was an eye-catcher, a well-built place, having a\
tall steeple adorning it. Mr. Crane had property on Main and 2nd Street.\
He was a Santa Fe engineer, later he was in the insurance and real estate\
business. He was tall, strong and wore a mustache. He was Temple’s\
second mayor. ;\
\
W.B. Farmer was a nice looking gentlemen, with short cut hair and\
mustache. He was active about town, having banking interests as well as\
other holdings. Mrs. Farmer was the former Lillie Keller.\
\
Along and about 1893, W.A. Wilkerson built a powerful three story\
building at 14th Street and Avenue D. It was located across the street in\
front of Cheeves Brothers. It’s now the eastside parking lot for the\
present Temple National Bank. For some time it was used as a United\
States Post Office. About middle ways of the long structure was a\
stairway to the second and third floor. Many different business concerns\
put up here, such as: Frank Hieronymus Printing Co., Shaffer Men's\
Store, a wholesale grocery company, newsstand, and, from tme to ume,\
other business enterprises. While upstairs were offices for real estate,\
Justice of Peace, Dr. J.B. Talley and et cetera, On the second floor, in the\
Justice of Peace Office, was the climax of a Temple lynching in 1915.\
~ O.W. Hasche was a partner in the Hasche & Whitefield Pens and\
Slaughter House. They kept the town well-supplied in fresh and dried\
~ beef. Most of their beeves were grass-fatened from surrounding\
\
106\
\
prairie-rolling pastures. They prospered and had nice dwellings.\
\
Dr. R.W. Barton’s home was two story, well-made and it also had a\
white picket fence. It was on 12th Street.\
\
J.B. Nunnelley was known for his white-headed mop of hair. He was\
one of Temple’s first business men and most successful.\
\
W.S. Banks, the former Belton lawyer, was always clean-shaven, tall\
man. His thick black hair fully covered his scalp. He and Mrs. Banks kept\
a pretty home.\
\
Other popular men about town were Harry Pye, N.H. Smith and John\
A. Cole. John Cole was in the insurance and real estate business. He had\
a brother Will Cole. John’s residence was 12th Street and Avenue A.\
\
George H. Gresham, ‘‘Big George’’, lived at 1606 West Avenue H. He\
had a nursery and was occupied with honey bees. He was one of\
Temple’s first volunteer firemen.\
\
Dr. C.T. Simpson was a stout, bald-headed man and wore a long, white\
bigote.\
\
The Southland Oil Mill began long ago on the MK&T tracks. It was\
first known as the Empire Oil Mill. This was in 1886. They boasted about\
processing 125 tons of seed cotton a day. This was some feat, so early.\
This plant has continued lo these many years without interruption as\
Temple’s oldest manufacturing plant.\
\
J.J. Booker, proprietor of the Booker Drug Store, lived in a nice\
dwelling at 904 South First Street. It’s now the Leamon Wall Realtors. As\
a young man, he was a will-o’-the-wisp, and very thin. However, he was\
attractive with a full head of hair and neat mustache.\
\
C.U. Yancey, Forrest B. Smith and L.C. Martin were two more\
nice-looking gents that were well-known men about town.\
\
The residence of Dr. W.L. Rogers, 14th Street and Avenue A, was\
another big two story set up, also having a pretty white picket fence.\
\
The dwelling of L.L. Fisher was one story and pretty as a picture. It had\
an iron fence around it. He was an alderman.\
\
A.F. Bentley was tall, slender, well-kenned and well-groomed. He was\
particular about his mustache; he loved bow ties; and he was just as\
smart as he looked.\
\
Rev. W.R. Maxwell was stout and strong. He had heavy hair and a\
thick beard. -\
\
On second street was the three story, gracious home of J.T. Smither.\
The J.T. Smither Compress & Co. was located at the cross tracks of the\
MK&T and the Santa Fe. It burned. Then it was built again some two\
blocks north of the crosstracks on 14th Street, alongside the MK&T.\
\
Second Street and Moore Avenue was the dwelling of Huling P.\
Robertson, mo\
\
Capt. George E. Wilcox Sr’s. three story near mansion stood\
prominent at 212 North 9th Street. Mr. Wilcox was a big powerful man.\
His black bow tie was always eminent being domed over by his striking\
bald head. He was one of the most progressive citizens to help spread the\

However, some girls bought goods from the peddler or town stores. Each
girl tried to be original in her design. Each exercised maticulous
‘craftsmanship with her busy fingers as she completed the finished
garment.

Oftentimes, in coolish weather, she wore lace mittens on her hands, a
long scarf over her shoulders, gold earrings and a gold brooch. Some
women like to part their hair in the center and pull it back over their ears
in a coiffure. Many, also, wore a flower or ornament in their hair.

In order to see the best dressed women you had to attend the opera, a
big camp meeting or go out to a square dance.

Many times parents would story to their girls about their ages. It was a
common belief that if a girl wasn’t married by the time she was sixteen,
that she wasn’t anything except a hard, toe-nailed, old maid. Still they
believed a gal had some chance until she was nineteen. Of course, there
were exceptions.

Some girls were proned to marry so soon, oftentimes the parents would
purposely set back some two or three years their daughter’s age. At
times, the parents, having storied about the girl’s age, later to die and
the girl would go through life in extreme doubt about her exact age:

And young men, approaching sparking age, took great delight in their
moustaches, keeping them well-larded and trained. They’d dress up in a
$6 suit, $1.25 pair of brogans, 75¢ shirt with E&W collar and cuff, 15¢
necktie, 10¢ pair of sleeve holders, 15¢ pair of galluses and top it off with
a $2.00 homburg or Kentucky derby, or 35¢ panama.

And, if he didn’t have his own rig, he’d go to the livery stable and hire
a $75 Banner buggy, pulled by a slick-haired horse that was worth at least
$200 and some much more.

Then he'd call on his sweetheart while she waited in the parlor of her
home. Oftentimes he’d take her a mess of flowers, bottle of cologne, box
of sweet smelling face powder and sometimes even a music box.

Now and then a few women, those that were on the skinny side, having
a bean pole figure padded themselves with cotton to look plump and
breasty. Those were scattered townswomen’s practices. In contrast the
old country gals were most often well-stacked. And in some cases the
country girls would specify to their fathers to use high sideboards on the
wagons whenever they visited Temple. Thereby a high sideboard wagon
could be used for their personal comforts. But most often those wagons
weren't too private since they leaked something awfully.

In and around the year of 1887 a newcomer could learn a lot by
listening to people talk. One elderly gentleman was talking about the
opening of a toll bridge that crossed the Leon River between Belton and
Temple. Since it was a stag session between whittlers and spitters he
commented:

‘‘People for miles around came over to cross on the new toll bridge. We
had fiddling and dancing and dinner on the ground. It was a magnificent
structure. Since it was on the San Antonio-Little Rock Stage many

120

dignitaries were on hand. At this very time it was one of the finest rock
and timber hook-ups that ever topped a stream borrow none. Towering
steel poles held enormous northern made cables. Rock pillars on both
sides of the embankment supported the oak runners.”’

‘This bridge my friends,’’ continued the Old Timer, was one of the
greatest bridges then known to Texas.”’

In another small group of men, the newcomer overheard the merits and
demerits of cottonseed. One farmer opinioned:

“Well, sir, I'll tell you right now. With these gins in full blast there’s a
bunch of old stray cows around there eating cottonseed. They ain’t got no
brand.”’

About that moment a farmer from Cockle Burr Flat exclaimed: ‘Well,
a cow that eats cottonseed, her milk ain’t any good, cause them seed
makes her milk greasy!”’

Then another old man from Reeds Lake near Little River acted like he
wanted to talk. He had been a good listener. He was gumming Brown
Mule tobacco.

Finally he just cut in:

You know what fellas? Last night me and Sadie got scired to death.
Two men rode up into the yard. My dogs shore did bark. I went out pretty
quick to see what wus up. You know I sleep with my clothes on. I left my
rifle behind the front door.

Wus they two thieves Seth?

‘I didn’t know what they wus. But they just spoke up and said they
wanted to stay all night.”’

“Well, the night looked so stormy I jist told ’em to pen their horses in
the corral and come on in. Soon they came in. They turned down supper.
Said Little River was busting out with wild game. I couldn’t get a good
look at ’em. I didn’t have any coal oil fur the lamps.”’

‘Did they act mean, cuss and sich?’’ asked one in the party.

‘‘No, but they wus rough talking. And then I told ’em to go to the shed
room where they'd find feather beds. When they went fur the shed room
the moon peeped out from a cloud and I noticed that each one had on a
big pistol.’’

“What you think then?’’ asked another friend.

‘Well, I began to think about Jessie James, Bill Longely and such.”’

‘‘What happened then?’’ questioned some fellow.

‘‘Then I just went on to bed. But I was wondering. About that moment
one of them lit a pine torch. And the other one began to read Jesus’
Sermon on the Mount. He read about 15 verses. Then the other one
offered a short prayer.

‘‘And then... . what happened?’’

‘‘And then me and Sadie turned over and went to sleep.”’

opened up a farm implement business in a big, long sheet iron building.

However, before this he opened up a hardware store and a machine
shop at 107 South Main Street, a few doors south of where the Temple
National Bank is.now.

Mr. Hollingsworth lived in a tiny village called Harrisville, a few miles
below Heidenheimer. The village was located on the edge of the Harris
farm. Mr. T.L. Hollingsworth’s son, Joe Hollingsworth, was born in the
village in 1881.

Mr. Hollingsworth realized Temple's potentials quite early so he
moved into town to cast his lot with the Prairie Queen in 1882.

And once that long sheet iron building was completed he began to sell
wagons. Such wagons as: Peter Schuttle, Studebaker, Mitchell and
Doering binders as well as John Deere implements. He did a thriving
business.

It might be well to treat two prominent men in this spell of time. They
were J.B. Nunnalle and L.M. Chattin. Both men were soon to become
very active in Temple’s business affairs as well as son Joe Hollingsworth.

At Harrisville, Nunnallee became a partner with Hollingsworth in their
general store.

L.M. Chattin was quite active since he carried mail by horseback and
back from Belton to Calvert.

Whenever Hollingsworth came to Temple, Nunnallee and Chattin
came about the same time. At Avenue B and 12th Street (Main) Nunnalle
& Chattin opened a general merchandise store. They did a wonderful
business. They are catalogued among Temple's first merchants and men
about town.

They both built fine homes and became rich. Cahpman was a large
man. He married a Rountree girl and moved to Florida.

At the corner of 17 and North 2nd Street was the home of the Roupe
family. At the present this is the new business firm of Western Auto
Associate store.

Between the Roupe dwelling and Hollingsworth’s sheet iron
implement building (102 East Central Avenue) was the home of Dr.
Knight. The aforementioned high wheel Holster car with the stick guide
that dashed over town at 10 and 15 miles per hour scaring the wits out of
men and beasts was owned by Dr. Knight. This was in 1904.

The National Laundry was first located at 103 South 1st Street, before
they moved to 102 South 5th Street, after the big opera house was
removed.

Away yonder before the Veteran’s Cafe came to being at 108 South
First was the site of the Prairie Queen Laundry. After the Prairie Queen
Laundry discontinued Bill Holden ran a restaurant and bar.

Bill Holden was pretty popular, a man about town. He was a railroad
man, then came his duty with the Gulf, Colorado and Santa Fe Hose
Company No. 110 in 1890. This company consisted of some 21 men. Bill
always claimed that, ‘‘me and the boys were mean to fires.’’

Bill Holden’s Restaurant and Saloon

Bill Holden was a brother to the well-known Miss Kitty Holden of 502
South Ist Street. She was a piano teacher for many years. There isn’t any
way of knowing how many students she taught. When she was in her
deep eighties, this writer used to visit with her. And she'd always go in
and play a number or two as a token of friendship. She’d also show old
photographs and talked about old time things. And those old time things
were actually old Temple history. She and Bill help make it.

A town “‘kid’’ that was ‘‘raised’’ on the streets of Temple was Grady
Jenkins. He received a small inheritance but like other Temple lads he
got out and worked. |

His Uncle, John L. Jenkins, bought two more lots at 115th and 117th
North 5th Street. He also owned the two story building at 219 West
Central Avenue, and the building at 12 South Ist Street, now Stephen's
Optometrist.

Grady Jenkins being the nephew of John Jenkins inherited those three
parcels of land. This gave Grady a little nestegg.

Somewhat past the town’s takeoff the Salvation Army came in. Of
course it was (is) a worthy outfit. This religious body bought out John
Jenkin’s two story building at 219 East Central Avenue.

They didn’t pay very much down. A note was drawn. Some worthy
Temple men went on the note, such as: Will Brown, Louis Bryant, A.A.
Stavinoha and one or two more. When the note came due there wasn't

We had some brief contact concerning the two Samuel Nunneleys. They are quite a\
mystery. Samuel (1842-1872) is supposed to have married Rachel Elizabeth Bowman,\
and, I doubt that there was a marriage. Samuel and Rachel had a child, Samuel\
Nunneley (1870-1924) who married Mamie Dorcus Lowry. Before Sam and Mamie\
married, it appears that Sam (1870-1924) had a son with Martha Jane "Mattie" Fanning.\
This child, Chester Arthur Nunneley was born 9 Mar 1897 and died in Caney,\
Montgomery Co. KS in 1956. The family of Chester have always known that Samuel\
and Martha were the parents and probably did not marry.\
\
Rachel Elizabeth Bowman is also, quite a mystery. She, apparently had two\
sons....James Auslam and Samuel Nunneley. On the 1870 Census of Madison Co. AR,\
she is listed as Rachel Taylor (living next door to James E. and Matilda Bowman, her\
parents. Rachel has two sons in the household, James Taylor (4) and Samuel Taylor\
(8/12). When Samuel died in 1924, his obit lists a half-brother, James Auslam and an\
aged mother, Elizabeth Graham.\
\
I have several obits, census records, etc., for the family of Samuel Nunneley (1870-\
1924). The big search is for the burial of Samuel (1842-1872) and his exact death date.\
It seems no one really knows what happened to Sam Houston Nunneley, only that he\
died in 1872. His death date was taken from the list of births and deaths of the children\
of Joseph Alexander Nunneley, prepared by a grandson of old Joseph (I have a copy).\
Mary R M Nunneley\

Judge Ben Lindsey of Denver,\
Colorado; Former Governor Henry J-\
Allen, of Kansas; and Colin O'More,\
famous: tenor with tae Chicago. Civic\
Opera Company ate three outstand-\
ing attractions for the 1924 Univer-\
sity-Fayetteville Lyceum’ Course\
agreed upon last night and this morn-\
ing by the lyceum joint committee.\
The committee - held’: + preliminary\
session-at the Washington Hotel last\
night and a “selection meeting” at\
tne University today,\
\
The course will cost approximate-\
ly $2500 with u guarantee of $2200.\
Other numbers may include Alton\
Packard, cartoonist; the Yedler Sym-\
phonic Quartette, and the McCarren\
Male Quartette.\
\
The present coursa hag brought\
here grund opera singers, and will\
bring such noted numbers as Taerlew\
Lieurance, composer of Indian music,\
and the Russian Cathedral Quartelte,\
said by many to be the most noted\
group of Russian musicians in the\
country.\
\
The Russian Singers will be here\
February 22nd, and Licurance und\
Company, April 22nd, The success\
of the present course has encouraged\
the committce to sign up for next\
season, A special price to all\
courses jy made to University stud-\
ents. Unless patréns prefer some\
otner system to reservation of seats\
this year’s. plan will continue,\

ALVARADO, Texua, Feb, 14—\
Louise Lawson, who was found\
choked to death by a gag placed in\
her mouth in her luxurious New\
York upartment, was buried here to-\
day, The town was filled with hun-\
dreds of outsiders who came to at-\
tend the ‘funeral’ services, All\
stores closed during the ceremony.\

CO; T0 MEET MAR 1

The Co-operative Farmers Mutual
Tornado and Windstorm Insurance
Company has just been orgunized
here, territory to be comprised in-
cluding Washington, Crawford, Ben-
ton, Carroll und Madison counties, A
second meeting will be held here
March Ist to perfect plans,

Organization was effected at a
mectiny called by the Washington
County Farmers’ Mutual Insurance
Company, to hear a report made by
hte investigation committee composed
of F. W. Letsch, G. Selle and H. 0.
Davis. A.constitution and by-laws
were adopted. At the March mect-
ing representatives from xll counties
in the district are expected and uv
poard of dircciors will be elected.

The following committee has been
named lo put everything in readiness
for actual and immediate operation:
W. D, Buker, Rogers; M. Y. Henson,
H, O. Davis, G. Selle, F. W, Letseh,
3. P. Harter and G. N. Weir.

“The new organization will effect
a great saving to the farmers oft
Northwest Arkansas und will also
permit them to carry added prolee
tion against (ornado and wind-
storms,” H, 0, Davis said today, “In
the past farmers have had ty pay ex-
cessive rates for this class of insur-
ance and many have not hid the pro-
tection because they felt they could
not afford to pay the high rate. It
is expected that this mutual company
will opernte on a cost plan, paying
approximatuly ynly ten cents per year
on ench $100) valuation of farm
property.”

CENTENNIAL HISTORY OF ARKANSAS 1069\
\
In 1854 Judge '’Maples was united in marriage to Miss Mattie McQuown, who\
wis born in Kentucky aud is a daughter of H.C. MeQuown, who removed to ‘Texas\
but later became a resident of Arkansas. Afler a short time, however, he returned\
to Texas, where bis remaining days were passed, his life being devoled to the work\
of the church as a iminister of the Methodist Mpiscopal church, South. Vo Judge\
and Mrs. Maples haye been boru six children, of whom tex aud Gip died in infatucy,\
The others are Ree, who is the wife of red Mowe, a farmer of Carroll county;\
Muth, at home; Katherine, the wite of Troy VPeiee, Mving on a farm in Carroll\
county; and Joe, a daughter, who is in the University of Arkansas at Payetteville.\
The Judge and his wife are members of the Methodist tpiscopal church, South, wud\
he is also a loyal follower of Masonry, being a Royal Arch Muson und a past master\
ot his lodge, He likewise belongs to the Independent Order of Odd Pellows and hus\
served its noble grand. He has membership with the Kuisghts of Pythias and with\
the Auti-llorse-Vhief Association und he gives hia political allegiance to the demo-\
cratic party, Jle hag been active in Politics from cacly manhood and has done cone |\
siderable camipuign work. Tle has attended various xliate conventions as a delegate\
and Wis epiniots have lou carricd wolght in the gouncils of his party. . ln fact\
he has been an influential factor in connection with public uffuirs along many lines -\
and has imade valuable coutribution to the progress and development of this section\
of tho slate, Aside from his farming interests he is now one of the directors of the\
Kirst National Bank of Berryville, and his cooporation can at all times be counted\
upon to further any plan or measure for the Benorab pao,\

In 1854 Judge Maples was united in marriage to Miss Mattie McQuown, who

democratic party and was a delegate to the national convention which nominated
Woodrow Wilson at Baltimore. He was also appointed deputy Internal Revenue Col-

financial condition by his capable management and business ability and today he

cess that has come to him by reason of the commendable methods he has followed
in its attainment.

Joe DeWayne Nunneley\
\
Funeral services for retired\
specialist E4 Joe DeWayne\
Nunneley, 27, formerly of rural\
Caney, Kan., will be held at 2\
p-m. Monday, July 25, in the\
Caney Valley High School audi-\
torium.\
\
Nunneley was born June 12,\
1967, in Coffeyville, Kan., to\
John Dewey and LaDonna Sue\
(Hicks) Nunneley, Sr. He at-\
tended schools in Caney and\
graduated from Caney Valley\
High School in 1985.\
\
He enlisted in the U.S. Army\
in February 1986, graduating\
from Fort Knox, Ky., in 1986. In\
March 1986 he received his AIT\
medical specialist training at\
Fort Sam Houston in San Ant-\
onio, Texas. There he received\
Army commendation medals for\
pharmacy technician for his\
dedication to medical duties\
and his vast knowledge of med-\
ical supply operations. His duty\
Station from June 1986 was\
Fort Ord in Monterey, Calif.\
\
In August 1988 he re-enlisted\
in the U.S. Army and changed\
his MOS to veterinary food in-\
spection specialist at Fort Sam\
Houston, where in June 1989\
he graduated as the distin-\
guished honor graduate and\
was his class leader during\
training. He was then assigned\
to duty station Presidio in San\
Francisco. In July 1989 he re-\
ceived a certificate of achieve-\
ment for outstanding perfor-\
mance of duty. His last certifi-\
cate of achievement was for ex-\
ceptional meritorious — service\
during recovery operations fol-\
lowing the San _ Francisco\
earthquake of October 1989.\
\
Following his auto accident in\
December 1989, he was medi-\
cally retired in April 1991 at\
Fort Riley, Kan.\
\
Nunneley was also a member\
of the Church of God in Knox-\
ville, lowa.\
\
Chapman, Kan.; one _ sister,\
Cindy Nunneley of rural Caney,\
Kan.; three brothers, John Jr.\
“Doc” Nunneley and Jim Nun-\
neley, both of Mt. Vernon, Mo.,\
and Jay Dee Nunneley of\
Chapman, Kan.;_ sister-in-law\
Jana (Yearout) Nunneley of Mt.\
Vernon; a special friend, Bernita\
Martin of San Francisco; several\
nieces, nephews, aunts and\
uncles.\
\
Graveside services will be held ©\
at 1 p.m. Tuesday, July 26, at\
Joe Nunneley’s countryside es-\
tate near Chapman, Kan.\
\
Family visitation will be July\
24 at the home of Gene Nun-\
neley, a resident of rural Caney,\
Kan\
\
The family suggests memori-\
als to the Kansas Head Injury\
Association, which may be left\
with the Graves Funeral Home\
of Caney, which in charge of the\
arrangements.\

Subj: Re: Nunneley\
\
Date: 3/25/04 11:37:48 AM Central Standard Time\
From: MILWILEST\
\
To: Nunne002\
\
OH! , YOU DO GOOD GIRL...... Have to say | dropped the ball on our line in favor of "going" way back & coming forward.\
Guess | figured you were still doing a great deal &\
\
probably had all this except for Mahulda.. | was so thrillled to such know where they went that | could get back to them later.\
As to info on John R., | basically had his land record.\
\
Have not been able to be in the office for awhile but they brought me your OK. book and |\
\
am so appreciative. | gave it to the guys to read and the picture of JB is so much better\
\
than what we had in his "costume" from the Order. Would you like me to share the expense of having John's research done in\
Ca.? Will be glad to. What a find. Tell me how you came about it - | haven't gotten to searchingCa. before except people\
entering by ship & maybe a few other items.\
\
While | wasn't out & about, | went thru the Ok. book & marked all the family "familiar" family names. The connections were\
always constant thru out the years. Using that & the maps really has helped to be convinced where & when they entered &\
moved along thru the states. It's when people like John & Rhoda strike out so far away that leaves some holes. There was\
another family of Nunneley's who appeared in those states....Wa,\
\
Or., but have to look them up to see what | have.\
\
Do you have anything on Eliza J. Nunneley & her children? If not | will send what | have:\
\
ADAM R. "JOHN" WOLF & ELIZA JANE NUNNELEY, married Oct 4, 1860 in Franklin\
\
Co.,Ar. Children: WILLIAM b: c.1862 prob. Franklin Co.,Ar.; CHARLES, b: c.1865 in\
\
Butler Co., Oh.; JENNIE, b: c.1866 in Butler Co.,Oh.; & CATHERINE, b: c.1868 in Butler\
\
Co.,Oh. - 1870 Census, BUTLER CO., OH., Middletown, Lemon Twp., 3 Aug 1870, p.121-122, dwelling/family #927/978 -\
JOHN WOOLF, age 34, Journeyman Carpenter, $5599 R.E.Value & $9175 Pers.Value, b: OH.; ELIZA, age 25, Keeping\
House, b: AR.; WILLIAM, age 8, Going to School, b: AR.; CHARLES, age 5, b: AR.; ELIZA JANE "JENNIE", age 4, b: AR.;\
CATHERINE, age 2, b: AR.,; JACKSON NUNNELLY, age 30, Photographer, $700 Pers.Value, b: AR.\
\
ELIZA JANE "JENNIE" married THOMAS LOUIS FORSYTH b: 21 Mar. 1860 in PERRY CO., OH. They m: 21 Apr 1881 in\
WELLS CO. Bluffton, IN., d: 21 Mar 1900 in Monroe\
\
Twp.,Upland, GRANT CO..,IN., buried: Masonic Cem., VAN BUREN, GRANT, IN. - his\
\
parents were SAMUEL & HARRIET DURR FORSYTHE. ELIZA JANE "JENNIE" WOLF\
\
FORSYTHE b: 1863, d: 21 May 1941 in ST.FRANCISVILLE, LAWRENCE, ILL., buried: Masonic Cem. Their children:\
HARRIET, b: 12 Aug 1881, d: 1 Sept 1946, marr: ADAM BECK on 7 Jan 1900; DORA B. b: 1889, d: 1 Feb 1960, marr:\
GEORGE BEATTY; ERVIN, b: 29 Dec 1890, d: 29 Sep 1967, marr: HAZEL MAY ISENBARGER (they divorced\
\
1 Aug 1926), he then marr: DELLA FRY; MAUDE, b: 21 Apr 1893, d: 14 Feb 1948, marr:\
\
PAUL RITTER on 25 Dec 1911; EDITH, b: 20 Jan 1894, d: 19 Sep 1974, marr: EARL ELSEA; NELLIE, b: 21 Oct 1898, d: 14\
Nov 1976, marr: CHARLES LYON; LOUIS, b: 7\
\
Mar 1901, d: 29 Jul 1953, marr: FLORA PARSON; BESSIE, b: 13 Jul 1902, d: 21 May 1974, marr: VERLIN BRAGG. (Will\
send the rest by snail mail.)\
\
Now, a marriage record for: ELIZA WOLFE to F.C. ECHOLS in CRAWFORD CO.,AR. on\
6 Mar 1879.....any idea who she belong to?\
\
Hope this makes sense....look to hear....Donna Miller\

Eliza -Thomas L. Forsythe married 20 Apr. 1881 in Bluffton , In. he was
born 21 Mar. 1860 in Perry Co. Ohio and died 6 May. 1909 in Monroe Twp.
Upland, In. he died of Cirrhosis of the liver, his parents were Samuel
Forsythe and Hattie Derr.

They had 8 children

Harriet

Page 1 of 1

Plan Services For Mrs. Beck

Van Buren, Ind., Sept. 3-Arrangements have been completed for funeral
rites for Mrs. Harriett B. Beck, sixty-five, wife of Adam Beck, for 2 p.m.
Wednesday at the Methodist church. She died at 11 p.m. Sunday at her home
in Milo after an illness of 11 months.

Rev. J. F. Sexton, pastor, will be in charge of the services. Burial will
be in the Van Buren cemetery.

A resident of Van Buren for more than 45 years, Mrs. Beck was the daughter
of Mr. And Mrs. Thomas Forsythe.

Survivors include the husband, four daughters, Mrs. Lela Herring and Mrs.
Rena Nash, both of Warren; Mrs. Donna Parson and Mrs. Beatrice Smith, both
of Marion; two sons, Harold and Leroy, both of Van Buren; five sisters,
Mrs. Dora Beatty, Sapulpa, Okla., Mrs. Eda Elsea and Mrs. Ella Dewalt,
both of Marion; Mrs. Bessie Bragg, Converse, and Mrs. Maude Chappell,
Michigan, and two brothers, Irvin and Lewis Forsythe, both of Illinois.

September 1946

Harriett B. Beck Services Planned

Van Buren, Sept. 3.—Funeral services for Mrs. Harriett B. Beck, 65, who
died late Sunday at her home, Milo, will be held at 2 p.m. Wednesday at
Van Buren Methodist Church.

Rev. J.F. Sexton, pastor, will be in charge of services and burial will be
in Van Buren cemetery.

Survivors include the husband, four daughters, Mrs. Lela Herring and Mrs.
Rena Nash, both of Warren; Mrs. Donna Parson and Mrs. Beatrice Smith, both
of Marion; two sons, Harold and Leroy, both of Van Buren; five sisters,
Mrs. Dora Beatty, Sapulpa, Okla., Mrs. Eda Elsea and Mrs. Ella Dewalt,
both of Marion; Mrs. Bessie Bragg, Converse, and Mrs. Maude Chappell,
Michigan, and two brothers, Irvin and Lewis Forsythe, both of Illinois.

Marion, Indiana, Chronicle June 27, 1960

Deaths and Funeral Services
John Manning

\
Descendant Register, Generation No. 2\
\
2. Harriett Forsythe (Thomas F orsythe!) was born 12 AUG 1881 in Wells County, Indiana, and\
died 1 SEP 1945 in Van Buren Tshp, Grant County, Indiana. She was buried in Van Buren\
Cemetery, Grant County, Indiana. She married Adam Beck 7 JAN 1900, son of Eli Beck and\
Nancy Jane Cloud. He was born 1 JUL 1880 in Huntington County, Indiana, and died 29 JAN\
1969 in Van Buren Tshp, Grant County, Indiana. He was buried in Van Buren Cemetery, Grant\
County, Indiana.\
\
Child of Harriett Forsythe and Adam Beck is:\
\
+3 ji, Lela N. Beck was born 28 JAN 1903 in Huntington County, Indiana, and died 29 JAN\
1969 in Bluffton, Wells County, Indiana.\
\
Descendant Register, Generation No. 3\
\
3. Lela N. Beck (Harriett Forsythe?, Thomas Forsythe!) was born 28 JAN 1903 in Huntington\
County, Indiana, and died 29 JAN 1969 in Bluffton, Wells County, Indiana. She was buried in\
Woodlawn Cemetery, Warren, Huntington County, Indiana. She married Norval Monroe Herring\
23 OCT 1926, son of Daniel Elmer Herring and Rebecca J. Bricker. He was born 14 MAR 1906 in\
Wells County, Indiana, and died 25 APR 1977 in Wells County, Indiana. He was buried in\
Woodlawn Cem., Huntington County, Indiana.\
\
Child of Lela N. Beck and Norval Monroe Herring is:\
4 i. Living Herring.\
